The sound of the torpedo hits was however not caused by her attack but by an attack by the Axum which hit three ships, HMS Nigeria, HMS Cairo and the tanker Ohio.

HMS Nigeria had to turn back to make for Gibraltar escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Derwent, HMS Wilton and HMS Bicester. Rear-Admiral Burrough transferred his flag to the destroyer HMS Ashanti. The stern of HMS Cairo had been blown off and she had to be sunk as she was beyond salvage with both engines also out of action. She was scuttled by HMS Pathfinder. The Ohio meanwhile managed to struggle on.

At this time the convoy was still trying to form up the the submarine attacks messed things up and right at thus time the convoy was once more attacked from the air in the growing dusk at 2030 hours. About 20 German aircraft, Ju-88’s made dive bombing and torpedo attacks, hitting the Empire Hope with a bomb and the Clan Ferguson and Brisbane Star with torpedoes. The first of these ships had to be sunk (by HMS Bramham, the second blew up but the last eventually reached Malta. Soon after this attack, at 2111 hours, HMS Kenya was torpedoed by the Italian submarine Alagi. She was able to evade three of the four torpedoes but was hit in the bow by the fouth. She was however able to remain with the convoy.

The situation was then as follows. HMS Kenya and HMS Manchester with two merchant ships, and with the minesweeping destroyers HMS Intrepid, HMS Icarus and HMS Fury sweeping ahead, had passed the Skerki Channel and were steering to pass Zembra Island on the way to Cape Bon. HMS Ashanti, with Rear-Admiral Burrough on board was fast overhauling these ships. The other two destroyers HMS Pathfinder, HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury, were rounding up the remaining nine merchant ships. The escort destroyer HMS Bramham was also catching up after having escorted the single Deucalion until she sank.

On learing about the fate of HMS Nigeria and HMS Cairo, Vice-Admiral Syfret detached HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali to reinforce Rear-Admiral Burrough. It would take these ships several hourse to catch up with the convoy.

The main body of the convoy passed Cape Bon around midnight. Fourty minutes later enemy Motor Torpedo Boats appeared and started to attack. Their first victim was HMS Manchester which was torpedoed at 0120 B/13 by the Italian MS 16 or MS 22. She had to be scuttled by her own crew. Many of her ships company landed in Tunisia and were interned by the Vichy-French but about 300 were picked up by destroyers (first by HMS Pathfinder, and later by HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. These last two destoyers then set off towards Gibraltar.)

Four and possibly five of the merchant ships were also hit by the Motor Torpedo Boats. These were the Wairangi, Rochester Castle, Almeria Lykes, Santa Elisa and probably the Glenorchy. They were attacked between 0315 and 0430 hours about 15 nautical miles south-east of Kelibia whilst taking a short cut to overhaul the main body of the convoy. Four were lost, only the Rochester Castle survived and she managed to catch up with the main body of the convoy at 0530 hours. The Glenorchy was sunk by the Italian MS 31, the other four, of which the Rochester Castle survived as mentioned earlier, were hit by the German S 30 and S 36 as well as the Italian MAS 554 and MAS 557.

Shortly before 0530 hours HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali had joined the main body of the convoy making the force now two cruisers and seven destroyers with the transports Rochester Castle, Waimarama and Melbourne Star. The damaged tanker Ohio was slowly catching up. With her was the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury. Astern of the main body was the Port Chalmers escorted by the destroyer HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Bramham. The destroyers recued the crew of the Santa Elisa when the passed by the abandoned ship which was afterwards finished off by a German bomber. The Dorset was proceeding without escort and lastly the damaged Brisbane Star was still keeping close to the Tunisian coast independently, intending to steer towards Malta after nightfall.

At 0730 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, sent back HMS Tartar and HMS Somali to Kelibia to assist HMS Manchester and then go to Gibraltar. When they arrived they found out that the Manchester had been scuttled several hours earlier so they rescued those of her crew that had not reached the shore yet and then made off to Gibraltar as ordered. Besides crew of the Manchester they also picked up survivors from the Almeria Lykes and Wairangi.

The next encounter with the enemy was an air attack on the main body of the convoy at 0800 hours by German bombers. About 12 Ju.88’s made a shallow diving attack coming down from 6000 feet to 2000 feet to drop their bombs. Two dived on the Waimarama hitting her several times and she blew up immediately, one of the bombers was even destroyed in the explosion. HMS Ledbury saved some of her crew out of the blazing sea. At 0925 hours, when the Ohio, Port Chalmers and Dorset where with the main body again, a few Ju.87’s escorted by Italian fighters attacked. They dived down to 1500 to 1000 feet. HMS Kenya leading the port column, and the Ohio last ship but one in the starboard column, had narrow escapes. One of the enemy aircraft crashed on board the Ohio just after having released it’s bomb after being damaged by gunfire from the Ohio and HMS Ashanti. Another aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by fighters from Malta that had been patrolling overhead since daybreak.
Arrivals at Malta 13-15 August 1942.

At 1050 hours, about 20 bombers, mostly Ju.88’s with a few Ju.87’s, came in to attack. Target was the Ohio and she received four or five near misses and her engines were disabled. At the same time the Rochester Castle in the port column was near-missed and set on fire but she continued with the convoy. The Dorset which was astern of her was hit and stopped. The convoy went on leaving the Dorset behind with the Ohio and two destroyers.

At 1125 hours the last air attack on the main body took place. Five Italian SM.79’s attacked with torpedoes and almost hit the Port Chalmers as the torpedo got stuck in the paravane. Further attacks on the main body were held of by fighters from Malta. At 1430 hours, four minesweepers from Malta joined the main body of the convoy, these were HMS Speedy (Lt.Cdr. A.E. Doran, RN, with the group’s commander A/Cdr. H.J.A.S. Jerome, RN on board), HMS Hebe, HMS Rye and HMS Heyte. Also with them were seven Motor Launches; ML 121, ML 126, ML 134, ML 135, ML 168, ML 459 and ML 462. HMS Rye and two of the ML’s were sent towards the damaged Ohio which was ‘vital for Malta’, according to A/Cdr. Jerome.

At 1600 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, set course to the west with his two cruisers and with five destroyers. The Port Chalmers, Melbourne Star and Rochester Castle arrived in Grand Harbour around 1800 hours with the force of A/Cdr. Jerome. The Rochester Castle was by that time very low in the water, she had just made it into port on time.

Out were still the Ohio, Dorset and the Brisbane Star. The valuable Ohio had been helpless with HMS Penn and HMS Bramham. When HMS Rye arrived at 1730 hours, HMS Penn took the Ohio in tow. Meanwhile HMS Bramham was sent to the Dorset but soon afterwards German bombers came again and the ships were attacked repeatedly until dark. Both merchantman were hit around 1900 hours and the Dorset sank.

At daylight on the 14th HMS Ledbury arrived to help bringing the Ohio to Malta. HMS Speedy also soon arrived on the scene with two ML’s. The rest of his force he had sent to search for the Brisbane Star. At 1045 hours, enemy aircraft made their last attempt, causing the parting of the tow. Fighter from Malta shot down two of the attackers. The tow was passed again and the slow procession went on and in the morning of the 15th the vital tanker finally reached Malta.

The Brisbane Star had by then also arrived. She left the Tunisian coast at dusk on the 13th. Aircraft had attacked her unsuccessfully and one of the attackers was shot down by a Beaufighter escort that had been sent from Malta. She arrived at Malta in the afternoon of the 14th.
Italian surface ships to operate against the convoy ?

The convoy had experienced the violence of the enemy in every shape except that of an attack by large surface ships. Yet Italian cruisers and destroyers had been at sea to intercept and attack it. Two light cruiser had left Cagliari in the evening of 11 August 1942 and the heavy cruisers Gorizia and Bolzano from Messina, and a light cruiser from Naples had sailed on the morning of the 12th. That evening reconnaissance aircraft reported one heavy and two light cruisers with eight destroyers about 80 nautical miles to the north of the western tip of Sicily and steering south. It would have been possible for this force to meet the convoy at dawn on the 13th so the shadowing aircraft was therefore ordered in plain language to illuminate and attack. This apparently influenced the Italians as they had limited air cover and they turned back at 0130 B/13 when near Cape San Vito. At 0140 hours the aircraft reported that it had dropped its bombs but no hits had been obtained. Similar orders were signalled, in plain language, to relief shadowers and to report the position of the enemy force to the benefit of imaginary Liberator bombers in case the Italians would change their minds and turn back. They however held on to the eastward.

The submarine HMS P 42 sighted them around 0800 B/13 off Stromboli and attacked with four torpedoes claiming two hits. She had in fact hit the heavy cruiser Bolzano which was able to proceed northwards and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo which managed to reach Messina with her bows blown off. The other cruisers went to Naples. Following the attack P 42 was heavily depth charged by the destroyers but managed to escape.

In fact the following Italian ships had been at sea; heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trieste, Bolzano, light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo. They were escorted by eleven destroyers; Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuceliere, Geniere, Legionaro, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Grecale and Maestrale.
The return to Gibraltar.

The British ships returning to Gibraltar had better fortune. Having left the convoy off Malta in the afternoon of the 13th, they rounded Cape Bon around 0130 B/14 and from that point until past Zembra Island they successful ran the gauntled of E-boats laying in wait.

at 0450 B/14, near the Fratelli Rocks, a submarine fired torpedoes at HMS Ashanti from the surface. She was nearly rammed by HMS Kenya, which was next astern of the ‘flagship’ (Rear-Admiral Burrough was still in HMS Ashanti). The inevitable shadowers arrived soon after daylight to herald their air attacks that began at 0730 hours. They lasted until around 1315 hours. German bombers came in first with three attemps by a few Ju.88’s. This was followed by a more severe attack with about 30 bombers, Ju-88’s and Ju-87’s between 1030 and 1050 hours. An hour later 15 Savoia high-level bombers attacked followed until 1315 hours by torpedo-carrying Savoia’s. Around 20 aircraft attacking single or in pairs. Also aircraft are though to be laying mines ahead. Several ships were near missed, but no further damage was sustained. After these attacks the British were left alone and in the evening they joined ‘Force Z’.

Vice-Admiral Syfret had gone as far west as 01’E where he ordered the damaged carrier HMS Indomitable to proceed to Malta with HMS Rodney and a destroyer screen made up of HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland. He then turned back to the east to make rendez-vous with Rear-Admiral Burrough. HMS Rodney, HMS Indomitable, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland arrived at Gibraltar in the evening of the 14th.

A few hours before they arrived the damaged HMS Nigeria and her escort had also entered port, as had HMS Tartar, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. On her way back HMS Nigeria had been attacked by torpedo-bombers and a submarine but she had not been hit.
Conclusion.

Out of the fourteen ships that had sailed only five arrived ‘safe’ at Malta. This was not a very high score also given the very heavy escort that had been provided also taken in mind that an aircraft carrier, a light cruiser, an AA cruiser an a destroyer had been lost and two heavy cruiser had been damaged. But the convoy had to meet very heavy air attacks by over 150 bombers and 80 torpedo aircraft, all in the space of two days. Also these aircraft were protected by fighter in much greater strength that the carriers and Malta could provide. And there were also the enemy submarines and E-boats.

The spirit in which to operation was carried out appears in Vice-Admiral Syfret’s report: ‘ Tribute has been paid to the personnel of His Majesty’s Ships, both the officers and men will desire to give first place to the conduct, courage, and determination of the masters, officers, and men of the merchant ships. The steadfast manner in which these ships pressed on their way to Malta through all attacks, answering every maneuvering order like a well trained fleet unit, was a most inspiring sight. Many of these fine men and their ships were lost. But the memory of their conduct will remain an inspiration to all who were privileged to sail with them.
Movements of the Italian Fleet during operation Pedestal.

The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli (7th Cruiser Division, Divisional Admiral Alberto Da Zara) escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Alfredo Oriani (leader) and Vincenzo Gioberti (10th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Cagliari at 1605 B/13-1625 B/13. They were sighted by HMS P 211 at 2020 B/13 in position 38°42'N, 10°29'E. They were attacked by HMS P 43 at 1840 B/13 and missed in position 38°46.5'N, 09°49'E. They arrived at Palermo at 0726 B/14-0826 B/14.

At 1910 B/14-2006 B/14, the squadron sailed from Palermo escorted by Alfredo Oriani, Ascari, Ugolino Vivaldi, Vincenzo Gioberti and Lanzerotto Malocello. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1745 B/14 to join them. After midnight, Vincenzo Gioberti had turn back due to defects and returned to Palermo at 0500 B/15.

At 0532 B/15, in position 190° - Punta Sciaccazza (Pantelleria) - 18 miles, The Italian squadron sighted two enemy cruisers and several destroyers of the Tribal and J/K/N-classes, bearing 220°, course 135°, 15 knots, at a range of 22000 metres. At a distance from them was a convoy consisting of a 12000-ton troop transport, a tanker and three merchant ships of about 6,000 tons, escorted by four Tribal-class destroyers. Immediately, Eugenio di Savoia catapulted a RO 43 seaplane and at 0536 B/14 (it later crashed at sea, the pilot was recovered by a MAS boat but the navigating officer was missing), Raimondo Montecuccoli also launched a RO 43 seaplane but it was forced by engine defects to abort the mission and landed in Sicily. The two cruisers increased speed to 28 knot and a couple of minutes later to 32 knots and opened fire with their 152mm/53 guns, with the third salvo straddling the enemy. The British gunfire soon straddled the Italian cruiser. At 0543 B/15, five enemy destroyers closed to attack, the leading destroyer was of the Tribal-class, followed by a J/K/N-class, the other three could not be identified. At 0546 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia opened fire on the leading destroyer with her main armament and as the range has now decreased, the 100mm/47 also joined. The Italian destroyers also opened fire at 0551 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres (their extreme range). Three minutes later, they were ordered to attack the convoy which was observed to turn back while enemy destroyers laid a smoke screen. At 0555 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia was hit by an enemy round on the 70mm armoured belt on the starboard side, two ratings were killed and eight were wounded. In the meantime, at 0559 B/15, the Italian destroyers had closed the enemy convoy at 5800 metres and the enemy destroyers at 5000 metres. Ugolini Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello each launched two torpedoes. At 0605 B/15, a merchant vessel appeared to be hit by a torpedo. Seven minutes later, an enemy destroyer was hit and the explosion was followed by a dense smoke, she withdrew from the action. At 0600 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia fired one torpedo. At 0609 B/15, the Italian cruisers resumed their fire on the enemy cruisers at a range of 17000 metres but observation was difficult due to the smoke from the enemy ships. At 0620 B/15, the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi was hit by a round in the forward engine room which had to be evacuated, ten men were killed and eight wounded. Admiral da Zara ordered the destroyers to give her assistance. Alfredo Oriani and Ascari were unable to do so due to the enemy fire. Only Lanzerotto Malocello stood by the stricken destroyer, and laid smoke to cover her. At 0645 B/15, the enemy destroyers were now at 4500 metres and Ugolino Vivaldi fired two torpedoes at the destroyer with the letters H 68 clearly seen on her bows [This was HMS Foresight]. Shortly after, she managed to increase speed at 13 knots. The enemy cruiser was firing at a range of 19000 metres at the Italian destroyers which could not reply due to the distance. At 0659 B/15, the leading enemy cruiser appeared to have been seriously hit, and the escorting destroyers covered her with smoke. By 0717 B/15, the enemy formation had completely disappeared from view, but a big detonation was observed. At 0735 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi sighted Premuda, Ascari and Alfredo Oriani and the former joined to escort her. At 0757 B/15, a violent fire developed in Ugolino Vivaldi's forward engine room the forward magazine had to be flooded, and ammunition thrown overboard as a precaution. At 0919 B/15, Lanzerotto Malocello took her damaged sistership in tow and they proceed to Pantelleria. The enemy appeared briefly at 0801 B/15, and the cruisers resumed fire for a minute before contact was definitely lost. At 0935 B/15, an enemy bomber of the Beaufort type attacked Eugenio di Savoia, one torpedo missed astern and the other sank in mid-course. In the meantime, the cruisers were still in action with the enemy and, at 1156 B/15, a large explosion was observed with a white column of smoke of over 1000 metres in height believed to be from an enemy ammunition ship. At 1223 B/15, next to the enemy merchant ship on fire, two destroyers were observed and twelve minutes later, fire was opened on one of them at the range of 19500 metres. It was certainly hit and masked by smoke from the other destroyer. Another two destroyers were then sighted next to another merchant ship on fire and fire was opened on one of them at 1259 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres. The destroyer blew up at 1325 B/15 and sank rapidly. In the meantime, Alfredo Oriani and Ascari opened fire at a tanker at a range of 12000 metres which was at repeatedly hit at 1302 B/15-1304 B/15 and set afire. Italian bombers. At 1343 B/15, Alfredo Oriani launched a torpedo at the tanker, but it was only a muffled hit. At 1423 B/15-1437 B/15, six enemy bombers attacked Alfredo Oriani and Ascari; they were not hit. In the action, Eugenio di Savoia fired 838 rounds of 152mm rounds, 690 rounds of 100mm, 978 rounds of 37mm and 973 rounds of 13.2mm, Raimondo Montecuccoli fired 704 rounds of 152mm rounds, 368 rounds of 100mm rounds and 567 rounds of 37mm. The cruisers came under air attack at 1428 B/15 by enemy bombers and at 1600 B/15 by three torpedo bombers. One of the latter was shot down by Italian fighters. Shortly after, the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Premuda joined the cruisers. Premuda was detached and reached Trapani at 2105 B/15. The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli, escorted by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ascari reached Naples at 1000 B/16-1154 B/16.

The tribulations of Ugolino Vivaldi. She was proceeding to Pantelleria, towed by Lanzerotto Malocello and escorted by Premuda. At 0927 B/15, the towing cable broke and Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Premuda to take over the towing and, nine minutes later, four Swordfish bombers attacked Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, but they managed to avoid their torpedoes. At 0948 B/15, another Beaufort bomber missed astern the destroyer Ascari. At 1003 B/15, the destroyers Ascari and Alfredo Oriani rejoined the cruisers. At 1019 B/15, Premuda managed to pass a tow line to Ugolino Vivavli which was still on fire and were proceeding to Pantelleria, fifteen minutes later, they were sighted at long range by the Italian cruisers. At 1218 B/15, two minesweepers from Pantelleria joined them and were asked to provide all the available fire extinguishers and at 1400 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi dropped anchor at Scauri (Pantelleria). At 1640 B/15, she ordered Premuda and Lanzerotto Malocello to leave the formation and rejoin the 7th Cruiser Division. At 2000 B/15, The damaged destroyer was joined by the tug Salvatore I and the fire was finally extinguished an hour later. At 0728 B/16, Ugolino Vivaldi departed from Pantelleria towed by Salvatore I, but the tug had engine defects and could not proceed at more than 5 knots. They were escorted by the torpedo boats Castore and Polluce and the tug Terracina and reached Trapani at 0154 B/17. (19)
3 Aug 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel off Kuriat. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.
The target was most likely Vichy-French.

2220 B/3 - Sighted the lights of a vessel on a northerly course steering up from south of Kuriat Island and 6 nautical miles from the shore. Ten minutes later she was seen to be accompanied by a second vessel. When north of Kuriat they altered course to the west. It might be that they were Vichy-French but they were now outside territorial waters and travelling at night so it was decided to attack. [This was in accordance with the orders regarding ships proceeding outside French territorial waters.]

2340 B/3 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes at the target, by now seen to be a merchant vessel of about 7000 tons escorted by a torpedo-boat. No hits were obtained. The torpedo-boat boat then switched on a searchlight so P 44 dived.
0040 B/4 - Surfaced and set course to the eastward to return to Malta as all torpedoes had now been expended. (16)

4 Aug 1942 At 0237 B/4, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) passed her signal timed 0105 B/4 to the Vice Admiral Malta and the Capt. S.10. The signal contained the following; One merchant ship, one torpedo boat, 308° - Kuriat - 5 miles, course 270° at 2345 B/3. All torpedoes fired, expect to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 0500 B/6.
In a reply timed 1029 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following; Your 0105 B/4 acknowledged. You may expect a minesweeper at the end of the search channel about 0959 B/6.
In a signal timed 2342 B/4, HMS P 44 sent the following; Your 1029 B/4. Amend time of arrival to 1900 B/5.
This last signal was passed at 0056 B/5. (20)
5 Aug 1942 At 2045 B/5, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (also 3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1815 B/5.

In a signal timed 1045 B/5, the Capt. S.10 had sent the following orders to HMS P 44; In order to enter harbour before dark, make rendezvous with the minesweeper as early as possible after 1830 B/5. (21)
9 Aug 1942 Around 1100 B/9, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (also 4th in the Mediterranean).
She departed Malta together with HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN). The submarines were escorted out by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) until around 1330 B/9.
She was ordered to patrol south of Lampedusa.

Later P 44 was to form part of a line with HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN). They were to provide cover during the passage of the Pedestal convoy.
10 Aug 1942 At 0550 B/10, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted a small trawler or patrol vessels creeping eastwards close to the south of Lampedusa. (16)
12 Aug 1942 At 0001 B/12, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) patrolled to the north-east of Mahdia. Large numbers of fishing vessels were seen to the north and north-west.
At 0300 B/12, HMS P 44 left the area to proceed to position 35°44'N, 12°00'E which was the westernmost position in the patrol lone of submarines covering Operation Pedestal.
At 1900 B/12, HMS P 44 was 3 miles 130° from the ordered position.

At 2319 B/12, the enemy report, timed 2305 B/12, of an aircraft was received. This reported four cruisers and eight destroyers in position 39°06'N, 12°24'E (north-west of Ustica) course 180° at 20 knots.

[This referred to an Italian force made up of the heavy cruisers Bolzano, Trieste, Gorizia, light cruiser Muzio Attendolo and the destroyers Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuciliere, Geniere, Grecale and Legionario.]

At 2335 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2219 B/12 was received. [For the text of the signal see the other event for 12 August 1942]. HMS P 44 set course for her new patrol position, 35°15'N, 12°00'E. (16)

12 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 2219 B/12, the Capt. S.10 ordered the submarines in the patrol line; HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) to take up positions on patrol line D as had been specified in their patrol orders. (22)
13 Aug 1942 At 0407 B/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0345 B/13. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 13 August 1942.]
At 0510 B/13, smoke was sighted bearing 330° and shortly afterwards the glare of what was thought to be a burning vessel was seen.
At 0605 B/13, HMS P 44 surfaced in accordance with the orders.

At 0610 B/13, when 3 miles to the south-west of the patrol position on line 'D', two cruisers and four merchant vessels of the convoy were seen bearing 250° at a range of 10 miles. HMS P 44 altered course to 100°.

At 0645 B/13, a merchant vessel, escorted by destroyers was seen bearing 270° at a range of 8 miles apparently trying to regain touch with the convoy. HMS P 44 altered course to the north to keep clear.
At 0800 B/13, course was altered to 100° and speed set to 9 knots in accordance with the orders.

At 0805 B/13, the bridge of a destroyer was sighted bearing 320° at a range of 8 miles. This destroyer was alone and on an unexpected bearing. It was considered possible this destroyer was enemy which was shadowing the convoy or the cruisers. HMS P 44 dived.
At 0810 B/13, the destroyer was seen to be a Hunt-class escort destroyer. HMS P 44 waited with surfacing until it had passed.
At 0825 B/13, HMS P 44 surfaced. HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) was sighted bearing 080° at a range of 8 miles.
At 0835 B/13, HMS P 44 dived for four approaching JU.88's which came down sun.
At 0846 B/13, HMS P 44 surfaced and continued eastwards at high speed.
At 0918 B/13, HMS P 44 had to dive once more when an aircraft came towards.
HMS P 44 surfaced again.
At 1005 B/13, HMS P 44 dived upon having received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0826 B/13. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 13 August 1942.]
At 1050 B/13, a heavy and prolonged explosion was heard.

At 1600 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1227 B/13 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 44; Return to previous patrol through position 36°01'N, 12°01'E to area 'K', west of 12°30'E between 34°59'N and 35°45'N.

At 2130 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1723 B/13 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 44; etc.) Operation not yet completed, keep north-east of the line Gozo - Pantellaria until further orders.
Course was set to the north-east to patrol withing 30 miles from Pantelleria. (16)

13 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0345 B/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) were informed of the following; Convoy two hours late. Delay easterly movement on the surface until 0800 B/13 and dive at 1400 B/13.

In a signal timed 0826 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines of the following; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13. (22)

14 Aug 1942 At 1315 B/14, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1029 B/14 which contained the following orders for her; Proceed through 36°10'N, 11°59'E to operate temporarily west of 12°01'E between 36°15'N and 35°50'E. Submarines are not to be attacked outside these areas if encountered on similar courses. (20)

15 Aug 1942 At 1400 B/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), which was patrolling to the north of Kuriat, received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1121 B/15, in which she was ordered to patrol in position 35°22'N, 11°55'E, which was about 50 miles to the south-east.
At 1716 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1625 B/15 was received which contained the movements of an escorted merchant vessel.

At 2251 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2231 B/15 was received which contained further movements of the vessels referred to in his earlier signal timed 1625 B/15. [For the text of these signals see the other event for 15 August 1942.] (20)

15 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1625 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); One merchant ship and one destroyer in position 38°43'N, 12°56'E, course 205°, speed 15 knots at 1615 B/15. Aircraft may attack before 0200 B/16.

In a follow up signal timed 2231 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to P 34 and P 44; One merchant ship and two destroyers in position 37°25'N, 11°47'E, course 180°, speed 15 knots at 2200 B/15.
[For the composition and further movements of this convoy see the event for 16 August 1942.] (22)
16 Aug 1942 At 0100 B/16, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) commenced to patrol in the vicinity of the ordered position (35°22'N, 11°55'E) with listening watch closed up.
At 0545 B/16, HMS P 44 patrolled across the estimated mean line of advance of the reported convoy.
At 1200 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0930 B/16 was received in which HMS P 44 was ordered to return to her original patrol area focusing on position 35°36'N, 11°16'E.
The expected convoy had turned back and proceeded to Trapani.

The expected convoy was made up of the Italian transport Rosolino Pilo (8326 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Naples for Tripoli at 0615 B/15-0700 B/15. When it was learned that an important enemy convoy had sailed from Gibraltar (operation PEDESTAL), they were ordered to Trapani where they arrived at 0414 B/16-0605 B/16. They finally sailed from Trapani at 0530 B/17-0635 B/17. They were attacked by four Beaufort torpedo bombers from 86 Squadron (RAF), Rosolino Pilo was hit by a torpedo at 1550 B/17 in position 35°55'N, 11°49'E; Vincenzo Gioberti was machine-gunned and had about 50 wounded, including her Captain. She turned back and arrived at Trapani at 2200 B/17. Two MAS boats sailed from Pantelleria to assist the freighter. Maestrale was ordered to turn back and arrived at Trapani at 0316 B/18. The tug Montecristo had been ordered to take her in tow. Rosolino Pilo was finished off by a torpedo from HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) at 0009 B/18 [see the event of 18 August 1942 for more details]. There were no casualties. She carried 4000 tons of stores for the Germans with a civilian crew of 50 and a military crew of 32 Italians and 39 Germans, and 110 German passengers. She had already been abandoned by her passengers and crew before the submarine attack, except her captain and four crew. Members who had remained on board and were evacuated shortly before she sank. Montecristo picked up the survivors. (20)
17 Aug 1942 At 0015 B/17, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) commenced to patrol between Mahdia and Ras Dimass.

At 0315 B/17, the S.10's signal timed 0242 B/17 was received which ordered HMS P 44 to position 35°40'N, 12°01'E (west-north-west of Lampedusa) by 1359 B/17. Course was altered to comply with these orders and speed was increased to 11.5 knots.
Around 1100 B/17, HMS P 44 arrived in the position ordered. She had only dived at 0658 B/17.
At 1357 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1247 B/17 was received which reported the enemy convoy now near Pantelleria proceeding southwards.
At 1500 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1215 B/17 was received in which P 44 was ordered to return to the Kuriat area (35°40'N, 11°08'E) after 1800 B/17.
At 1510 B/17, a distant explosion was heard.
At 1550 B/17, two sets of two explosions were heard.
At 1750 B/17, two aircraft were seen four miles to the westwards. They were proceeding south.
At 1800 B/17, Lt. Barlow concluded that the enemy convoy had either slipped by or had been forced to turn back. Course was set to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1215 B/17.

At 1948 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1755 B/17 was received in which P 44 was ordered to investigate the scene of a successful RAF attack on the convoy. The reported position was 295° - Lampion - 16 miles.
At 2050 B/17, HMS P 44 surfaced. It was found that she had been set 6 miles to the south-west. Lt. Barlow decided to search along a bearing of 295° from Lampion to the westward.
At 2108 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1900 B/17 was received with gave the position of the damaged enemy transport as 70 miles to the south of Pantelleria.

At 2154 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2101 B/17 was received which ordered HMS P 44 to search to the north-west from position 285° - Lampion Island - 16 miles. This was now P 44's actual position but it was decided to hold on to the north until 2230 B/17 when flares would be dropped.
At 2234 B/17, the first flare was sighted bearing 315° a long way away. Speed was increased to 10 knots and course was altered to the bearing in which the flare was seen.
At 2239 B/17, a second flare was dropped. The damaged enemy transport was now sighted. More flares were dropped at 2246 B/17, 2250 B/17, 2303 B/17 and 2307 B/17.

At 2307 B/17, the damaged enemy transport was clearly visibile in the light of the last flare bearing 317° at a range of 6 miles. Speed was reduced. No HE was picked up and there was no sign of the escort. The transport was found to be stopped and down by the stern. She was of about 7000 to 8000 tons. Position was 302° - Lampion - 29 miles. (20)

18 Aug 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Rosolino Pilo (8326 GRT, built 1942) 50 nautical miles bearing 190 of Pantellaria. The Italian ship was already damaged in a previous attack by British aircraft. Rosolino Pilo was been escorted by the Italian destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti and was en-route from Trapani to Tripoli [See the event of 16 August 1942 for more info on this convoy's movements].

She had a civilian crew of 50, a naval crew of 32 Italians and thirty-nine as well as 110 German passengers. There were no casualties as they had evacuated long before P 44 finished the ship off. Only Captain Oreste Tomassi and four sailors had remained on board but these too had left just shortly before the submarine finished her off.

0009 B/18- Fired one torpedo from 1500 yards. The result was gratifying, if unexpected. The explosion of the torpedo was followed instantaneously by another of gigantic proportions as the whole merchant ship exploded. As the commanding officer left the bridge a violent hot blast reached P 44 and debris was already clattering on the casing and before it was possible to dive, a report of the motor room was received of water coming in fast. P 44 therefore remained on the surface as no escort seemed to be present and when the hailstorm of debris had descended, it was found that the bridge was partially wrecked by a 12 foot length of one-inch frame embedded in the starboard side. Jumping wire had parted and loop and main aerials broken. In addition, upper steering was wrecked. There was no sign of the merchant ship.
The pressure hull aft was examined and a 6" gash was found which was then caulked with wood and cotton waste.
At 0434 B/18 - A signal, timed 0047 B/18, was passed to the Capt. S.10 reporting the situation.
0441 B/18 - Dived to ascertain the water tightness of the submarine. The inflow of water could be kept under control up to 70 feet.
0545 B/18 - Patrolled between Lampion and Lampedusa awaiting a reply from the Capt. S.10.
1309 B/18 - Received orders to return to Malta (Capt. S.10's signal timed 1051 B/18). Set course accordingly. (16)
19 Aug 1942 At 1240 B/19, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (also 4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 0930 B/19. (16)

20 Aug 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) is taken in hand for repairs to the pressure hull and bridge at the Malta Dockyard. It was not needed for the submarine to be docked. HMS P 44 left the dockyard on 4 September. (23)
6 Sep 1942 Around 1700 B/6, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (also 5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Misurata.
She was to proceed through position 33°01'N, 15°29'E to area 'M' (31°10'N, 15°01'E - 000° - 110 - 80 ).
She was escorted out until around 1940 B/6 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).
Gunnery exercises were carried out on departure. (16)
6 Sep 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 44 during her 5th war patrol see the map below.

7 Sep 1942 At 0650 B/7, in position 34°25'N, 14°52'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) picked up HE bearing 280°. The HE faded out bearing 160° at 0720 B/7. Nothing was sighted. Visibility and Asdic conditions were good and the HE could have been a long way off. The rate of change of bearing and position would fit for a vessel proceeding direct to Tripoli at 25 knots from the region of Taranto. (16)
8 Sep 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) arrived in her patrol area and patrolled 15 miles to the north-east of Ras Zorug on the supposed direct route from Tripoli to Benghazi. (16)

12 Sep 1942 At 2150 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1053 B/12 was received by HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN). It contained the following orders; Patrol between Misurata and Khara during the forenoon of the 13th, from 0730 B/13.

At 2352 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2342 B/12 was received which contained the following information; One unescorted merchant ship in position 32°44'N 14°12'E, course 118°, speed 6.5 knots at 2330 B/12. (24)
13 Sep 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked the small Italian transport Ascianghi (610 GRT, built 1922) with two and later one torpedo. All torpedoes missed despite the claim for one hit.
Ascianghi was unescorted and on a trip from Tripoli to Benghazi which she reached on the 15th.
This was the vessel reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2342 B/12.
0925 B/13 - Sighted smoke bearing 270°. Went to the southward at speed for 60 minutes to intercept.
1030 B/13 - Mast and funnel were now distinguishable. Range could however not be estimated due to the mirage.
1112 B/13 - In position 32°24'N, 15°26'E fired two torpedoes from 700 yards. One hit was obtained. The ship stopped. P 44 retired into deeper water. The ship was abandoned and had started to list.
1132 B/13 - Decided to return to finish her off. Shortly afterwards the enemy got underway again and made off towards the shore.

1204 B/13 - P 44 had followed her and fired another torpedo from 2500 yards which missed. Decided to return after dark to finish her off with gunfire as it seemed likely she was going to beach herself.

1400 B/13 - Observed the enemy to be underway and steering south. She was now escorted by aircraft and it was only possible to keep up with her till dark. She was estimated to make direct for Sirte and aircraft accompanied her until 1845 B/13 by which time the merchant vessel was out of sight.
1845 B/13 - Surfaced and proceeded at 11.5 knots and searched along the anticipated course of the enemy.

2035 B/13 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1759 B/13 which contained the following information and orders; Enemy submarines are thought to be store carrying to Tripoli during moonless periods. If encountered they may be particularly vulnerable to gunfire if torpedo attack fails. Patrol 065° - 245° either side of position 33°04'N, 14°25'E from 0001 B/15 until 0601 B/15.

[This positioning must have been to intercept the Italian submarine Pietro Micca which had departed Taranto the previous day for a transport run to Tripoli. The British must have been aware of her passage due to an ULTRA intercept.]

2117 B/13 - Sighted five groups of three white Verey lights bearing 210° (in the direction of Buerat-el-Sun). P 44 altered course to investigate. It was considered that the merchant vessel was making for that port and the time of the lights coincided with her time of arrival there, allowing her 5 knots speed.
2330 B/13 - Closed Buerat-el-Sun to 3 to 4 miles range but sighted nothing.
2335 B/13 - Set course to the northward to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1759 B/13. (16)

15 Sep 1942 At 0001 B/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) commenced to patrol on courses 065° - 245° through position 33°04'N, 14°25'E as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1759 B/13.
At 0600 B/15, HMS P 44 set course to the southward to patrol on the shipping route.
At 2056 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1919 B/15 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol in vicinity of 29 miles 155° from the position given in my last signal from dawn/16.
This thus ordered patrol in position 33°38'N, 14°40'E which was P 44's actual position at the time the signal was received. (24)
16 Sep 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) patrolled all day in the vicinity of the position ordered (33°38'N, 14°40'E) in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1919 B/15.
At 2150 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1150 B/16 was received in which HMS P 44 received orders to leave patrol P.M./17. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (24)

17 Sep 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sank the Italian salvage vessel Rostro (333 GRT, built 1917) and the Italian auxiliary A/S schooner V 39/Giovanna (158 GRT, built 1910) east of Khoms, Libya.
0925 B/17 - Sighted masts of a sailing vessel bearing 252°.
0943 B/17 - Sighted masts of a steamer astern of the sailing vessel. The sailing vessel was by now seen to be a two-masted vessel proceeding under her own power. Started attack.
1031 B/17 - In position 32°40'N, 14°40'E fired two torpedoes from 1000 yards at the merchant vessel. One explosion was heard and HE of the target ceased. It started again 10 minutes later.

1050 B/17 - Observed the merchant vessel to be down by the stern heading for the shore. Shortly afterwards both vessels were seen to anchor near the shore. Decided to return after dusk to destroy them with gunfire.

1955 B/17 - Surfaced 2 nautical miles from the shore and ran in towards the enemy while firing with the gun. They were very difficult to make out against the land and this made the gun action very difficult. After a while decided to fire one torpedo against each ship.
2036 B/17 and 2037 B/17 - Fired a torpedo at each ship from 900 and 800 yards. Both ships were hit and sank at once in position 32°35'N, 14°24'E.
2040 B/17 - Retired to seaward at 11.5 knots and set course to Malta as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1150 B/16. (16)
19 Sep 1942 Around 1620 B/19, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (also 5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1345 B/19. (25)
28 Sep 1942 Around 1500 B/28, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (also 6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south Calabrian coast.

She was escorted out by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR). A practice attack was made on HMS Rye and also gunnery exercises were carried out by HMS P 44 before they parted company around 1815 B/28. (16)
28 Sep 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 44 during her 6th war patrol see the map below.

30 Sep 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel near Cape Spartivento, Calabria, Italy. A total of four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. This was the German transport Ruhr (5954 GRT, built 1925) which was narrowly missed. She was escorted by the italian destroyers Antonio da Noli and Freccia. The enemy convoy was proceeding from Messina to Patras.
0600 B/30 - Dived 10 miles to the south of Cape Spartivento. Continued to close the land.
0810 B/30 - Sighted an eastbound destroyer passing to the northward. The destroyer appeared to be on A/S patrol.
0847 B/30 - Went to 70 feet for an aircraft and to repair a defect in the high power periscope. Picked up HE bearing 300°.

0850 B/30 - Retured to periscope depth and sighted a 4000-tons merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers (one Navigatori-class ahead and one Maestrale-class on the starboard side). They were on an easterly course, hugging the coast. Range was 5 nautical miles. Started attack.
0920 B/30 - In position 37°52'N, 15°58'E fired three torpedoes from 3000 yards. No hits were obtained.
0945 B/30 - Fired one torpedo. This one also missed.
1100 B/30 - Patrolled between Cape dell'Armi and Cape Spartivento keeping 2 miles from the shore.

1151 B/30 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1012 B/30 which contained the following orders; Investigate position 38°14'N, 16°16'E where air reconnaissance shows a merchant ship aground after an air attack during the night of 28/29 September. If opportunity offers, torpedo her to prevent salvage.
1300 B/30 - The destroyer seen at 0810 B/30 was seen returning westwards.
1530 B/30 - Two motor torpedo boats were seen proceeding westwards close inshore.
1950 B/30 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1835 B/30 which contained the following orders; After carrying out my 1012 B/30 proceed to the north-east end of your area for 48 hours. (24)

1 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) torpedoed and (further) damaged the Italian transport Ravenna (1148 GRT, built 1888) near Locri, Calabria, Italy. The Ravenna was beached after air attack and a fire during the night of 29/30 September 1942.

0700 B/1 - Sighted the reported merchant vessel aground in the position stated in yesterdays signal from Capt. S.10. A motor torpedo boat was patrolling to seaward and a schooner was alongside. Also two aircraft were patrolling overhead. Tried to obtain a suitable firing position for nearly 2 hours.
0855 B/1 - In position 38°15'N, 16°17'E fired one torpedo from 2000 yards. Heard it hit.
0858 B/1- Fired another torpedo at a second vessel that had meanwhile been sighted half a mile to the south. This vessel was believed to be a tug or salvage vessel. This torpedo missed.
0940 B/1 - The merchant vessel was seen to be deeper in the water with the tug standing by. The schooner that had been alongside could no longer be seen, it is considered to have been sunk.
After the attack P 44 retired to the north-east end of her patrol area as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1835 B/30. (16)

2 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked the Italian water tanker Oristano (280 GRT, built 1914) with gunfire off Cape Colonne. The action was broken off when shore batteries (197th Battery, 76cm guns) opened up an accurate gunfire and the auxiliary submarine chaser Maria arrived on the scene.
0545 B/2 - Dived off Cape Colonne. There was a heavy sea and swell from the north-north-west.
1800 B/2 - When 2 nautical miles east of Cape Colonne sighted a small steamer of less then 500 tons rounding the cape from the south. Chased her inshore to engage with the gun.

1823 B/2 - To pursue her any further would bring P 44 too close to Crotone so surfaced at 2500 yards range and opened fire. After only 3 minutes, shore batteries opened an accurate fire. P 44 had fired 11 rounds and was just fining the range when the action had broken off. P 44 then retired to seaward. In the meantime two merchant vessels and several trawlers were seen to be present inside Crotone harbour. (16)
3 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) patrolled off Cape Rizzuto.

At 1445 B/3, two A/S patrol vessels from Crotone swept down the coast from Cape Colonne to Cape Rizzuto and disappeared on an approximate course of 220°. The vessels were a small 300-tons camouflaged trawler, followed a mile astern by a 750-tons coaster type of vessel painted dark grey. Both were transmitting.

At 2118 B/3, the Capt. S.10's 1213 B/3 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol through position 37°22'N, 16°20'E by reverse of outward route to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 1015 A/6. HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) due from the north-west at the same time and is to keep north-west and P 44 south-east of a line 245° from the end of the searched channel until surfaced to make rendezvous with the minesweeper. S.S./T. communication to be attempted from time entering sanctuary and HMS Clyde is to surface first. (26)
4 Oct 1942 At 0553 B/4, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) dived off Punta Stilo and patrolled southwards along the coast, examining a railway bridge and passing trains.
At 0700 B/4, a small schooner armed with a gun passed inshore on a northerly course.
At 1030 B/4, one motor torpedo boat passed southbound keeping close inshore.
At 1400 B/4, the damaged merchant vessel was examined again. It was now seen to be severely listed. Both the forward holds were blown open. A schooner was seen alongside her. No activity was seen.
At 1945 B/4, HMS P 44 patrolled southwards along the shipping route as far as Cape Spartivento.
At 2230 B/4, course was set to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1213 B/3. (16)
6 Oct 1942 At 1445 B/6, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (also 6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR), which had joined at 1130 B/6. (16)
13 Oct 1942 Around 1345 A/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (also 7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Misurata.
She was to proceed through position 35°21'N, 14°15'E about 2201 A/13 and then proceed towards her patrol area on course 165°.

She was escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) on which also a practice attack was carried out. Exercises were completed around 1800 A/13 when they parted company and P 44 proceeded on patrol. (16)
13 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 44 during her 7th war patrol see the map below.

13 Oct 1942 At 2247 A/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received a contact report of an aircraft which reported a convoy in position 34°55'N, 19°25'E at 2140 B/13. The enemy was steering to the south-south-west and most likely bound for Tripoli.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Amsterdam (10873 GRT, built 1921) which was on passage from Patras to Tripoli. She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Circe and Lince. (26)
14 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received further aircraft reports regarding the same convoy as in the aircraft report received at 2247 A/13.

In a report timed 0055 C/14, the following was reported; Three destroyers and two unknown in position 35°14'N, 20°03'E, course 250°, speed 10 knots. Have attacked the enemy with bombs level, result of attack unobserved.
In a report timed 0118 C/14, the following was reported; Position of enemy 35°08'N, 19°09'E.

At 1359 A/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1148 A/14 [another source give the time of this signal as 1158 A/14] was received which contained the following; Aircraft report, one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 33°59'N, 17°59'E, course 240°, speed 9 knots at 0718 A/14. Torpedo aircraft may attack tonight east of 15°01'E.
At 1624 A/14, 14 heavy but distant heavy explosions were heard. There were followed by 5 series of 6 to 8 explosions at 1632 A/14, 1705 A/14, 1715 A/14 and 1735 A/14.
At 1745 A/14, HMS P 44 surfaced and proceeded at 10 knots as it had been calculated that it would be just possible to get on the expected line of advance of the convoy around midnight.

At 1853 A/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1815 A/14 was received which gave the following information; Aircraft report, one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 33°05'N, 16°35'E, course 230°, speed 10 Knots at 1515 A/14.

At 2051 A/14, an earlier signal from the Capt. S.10 was received which must have been missed in an earlier W/T routine. This signal contained the following orders; Endeavour to be in position 32°52'N, 14°37'E by 0001 A/15.
HMS P 44 increased speed and set course to proceed to the ordered position.

At 2156 A/14, distant flares were sighted bearing 110° which fitted well with the expected position of the enemy convoy. Further flares were observed at 2220 A/14, 2245 A/14, 2307 A/14, 2322 A/14 and 2330 A/14. Lt. Barlow decided to steer southwards to intercept to the east of the ordered position. (26)

14 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1815 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); Aircraft report one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 33°05'N, 16°35'E. Course 230° at 10 knots at 1515 A/14.
In a signal timed 2326 A/14, the following orders were sent by the Capt. S.10 to HMS P 42 and HMS P 44; On completion of attack or at noon/15, P 42 operating in area 'N', P 44 in area 'M'.

The reported westbound ship and two escorting destroyers referred to the following convoy; At 0531 B/5-0600 B/5 October, the Italian transport Amsterdam (8673 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo and the patrol boats A.S. 211, A.S. 206 and A.S. 226 sailed from Naples and arrived at Messina at 0212 B/6. They sailed from Messina at 0415 B/7-0430 B/7, reinforced by the torpedo boat Circe (which took over from Perseo as the convoy leader). The three patrol boats were to scout ahead on an A/S search as far as Crotone until 1830 B/7. At 1117 B/8, an escorting Cant Z.501 dropped two bombs on a presumed submarine. Two minutes later, a German Junker 88 did the same. The freighter, averaging a speed of 9.5 knots, and the two torpedo boats arrived in Patras at 1906 B/8-1920 B/8. Initially, the convoy was to sail from Patras on 9 October with the torpedo boat Lince substituting for Perseo, they only sailed at 1615 B/12-1658 B/12. [The reason for the delay is not known to us]. At 0705 B/13, they were joined by the destroyer Bombardiere, which assumed command until 0804 B/14 when she left the convoy and returned to Navarino. Circe resumed duty as convoy leader. At 1715 B/14, Lince detected a submarine and attacked it with depth charges and searched the area before rejoining the convoy at 2045 B/14. At 2255 B/14, in position 034° - Misurata – 50 miles, a flare was observed, and a minute later an enemy bomber dropped a torpedo which passed between Amsterdam and Circe. At 0350 B/15, a bomber dropped two bombs near Amsterdam. At 0410 B/15, Amsterdam was hit by a torpedo from an enemy bomber in position 33°10'N, 15°02'E. She was abandoned by most of her crew, and Lince recovered the survivors. However, Amsterdam was still afloat, and Circe signalled Marina Tripoli to send a tug. In the meantime, Circe had managed to take her in tow and proceeded toward Homs. The tug Pronta (182 GRT, built 1913) escorted by the minesweeper RD 39 sailed from Tripoli at 0700 B/15 to their assistance and, at 1246 B/15, in 33°12'05"N, 15°01'E, the escort was reinforced by the arrival of the torpedo boat Calliope from Tobruk. Calliope was short of fuel and had to leave them at 1900 B/15 to refuel at Tripoli (0720 B/16-1310 B/16). She returned the following day at 1615 B. At 2120 B/15, Pronta had arrived and taken over the towing. In the meantime, Marina Tripoli had informed them that the trawler Scorfano (308 GRT, built 1924), the tug Porto Palo and the motor fishing vessel Sparviero (498 GRT, built 1915) were also dispatched to assist them, and they reached them at 1025 B/16. During the night of 15/16, Lince left them and arrived at Tripoli at 1125 B/16. At 1136 B/16, Porto Palo took Amsterdam in tow, and a few hours later Scorfano also helped with the towing. The tug Audax (115 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the minesweeper RD 31 had also sailed from Tripoli at 0930 B/16 and joined them at 1715 B/16, except RD 31 which had to turn back due to the rough seas. At 1828 B/16, Amsterdam was beached 600 metres from the Homs pier. Circe arrived in Tripoli at 0820 B/17 and Calliope at 0914 B/18. (27)
15 Oct 1942 At 0001 A/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) arrived in a position on the anticipated line of advance of the enemy convoy and commenced to patrol to the eastwards.
At 0035 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2326 A/14 was received which contained the following orders; On completion of attack or at noon on the 15th patrol in area 'M'.

At 0130 A/15, aircraft flares were sighted bearing 030° so turned north. At the same time an aircraft report timed 0028 A/15 was received which contained the following information; One merchant ship 'left' [???] destroyer 32°53'N, 15°45'E, course 244°, speed 8 knots at 2145 A/14. This reported the enemy 30 miles to the south. Lt. Barlow decided to ignore this report as it seemed incorrect to him.

At 0235 A/15, aircraft flares were sighted ahead and also an aircraft report giving the convoy's position at 0100 A/15. This report contained the following; Am over one motor vessel and two destroyers in position 33°09'N. 15°29'E, course 260°, speed 10 knots.
At 0255 A/15, flares and HA tracers were seen bearing 037° apparently some way off. Lt. Barlow decided to keep ahead of the enemy and to intercept at dawn.
At 0508 A/15, P 44 dived to await the convoy.
At 0648 A/15, nothing had been sighted yet. Lt. Barlow considered the convoy must have altered course to the south so he ordered P 44 to patrol to the south-eastward.

At 0935 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0845 A/15 was received which contained the following orders; Proceed forthwith to investigate position 33°09'N, 15°29'E where westbound ship reported stopped after air attack at 0101 A/15.
Course was set accordingly.

At 1205 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0932 A/15 was received which contained the following information; My 0845 A/15. Position at 0730 A/15 was 32°20'N, 15°50'E, stopped with a destroyer circling.
This was to the south in the Gulf of Sirte. Lt. Barlow decided to continue to the earlier reported position first.
By 1713 A/15, there had be no sign of the stopped ship. Smoke was now sighted to the southward apparently eastbound. Course was altered to close this smoke.

At 1745 A/15, HMS P 44 surfaced for a sight and to try to get a visual on the target while on the surface. Two small ships on an easterly course were seen at a range of 5 miles. P 44 dived again to avoid being sighted as it was still too light to close on the surface.
At 1830 A/15, HMS P 44 surfaced again and commenced to chase the enemy which was now five miles away on a 150° track.
At 1845 A/15, a vessel were seen to the northward. The two small vessels were still proceeding eastwards.

At 1851 A/15, this single vessel was seen to be a destroyer which turned towards. P 44 dived but no attack developed. The destroyer passed overhead at 1910 A/15 and disappeared on course 245° at 20 knots.
At 1945 A/15, HMS P 44 surfaced. There was no sign of the small vessels sighted earlier. A battery charge was now commenced while patrolling to the eastward.

At 2058 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1941 A/15 was received which contained the following orders / information; Investigate position 32°03'N, 15°28'E where aircraft reported ship stopped heading 175° around 1715 A/15. Pilot states ship is medium size and no other vessel in vicinity.
The reported position was about 65 miles to the south. (26)

16 Oct 1942 At 0515 A/16, it was found that HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) had been set to the westward during the last 36 hours. Course was set towards the last reported position of the stopped merchant vessel.

At 1830 A/16, HMS P 44 surfaced south of the expected position of the damaged merchant vessel. She then commenced to patrol to the northward keeping 2 to 3 miles from the shore. Nothing was sighted though. (16)
17 Oct 1942 At 0015 A/17, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) turned south to reach yesterdays surfacing position by dawn.
At 0520 A/17, got as far south as Buerat el Sun but nothing was sighted.

At 1750 A/17, HMS P 44 surfaced and proceeded to the north-west end of the patrol area. Still nothing had been sighted. [The damaged Italian transport Amsterdam (10873 GRT, built 1921) had been grounded of Khoms.]
At 2030 A/17, The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1251 A/17 was received. For the text of this signal see the other event of 17 October 1942.

It was not possible to reach the ordered position by dawn on the 19th. Lt. Barlow therefore decided to proceed to position 33°41'N, 13°03'E instead as had been suggested in the signal as alternative if unable to comply. (26)

17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1251 A/17, Capt. S.10 sent the following submarines HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) the following orders; Previous orders for Utmost, P 37, P 42 and P 44 are cancelled. Proceed to be in following positions by dawn on the 19th. HMS Utmost to position 36°16'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 36°47'N, 13°10'E en-route. HMS P 37 to position 35°45'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 35°40'N, 12°54'E en-route. HMS P 42 to position 35°25'N, 12°21'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 13°55'E en-route. HMS P 44 to position 35°12'N, 12°33'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 14°35'E en-route. HMS P 44 if unable comply proceed through position 33°41'N, 13°03'E and steer 343° keeping south of 35°10'N. (27)

18 Oct 1942 At 1200 A/18, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) was in position 32°44'N, 14°50'E when she passed two empty merchant ship type life saving rafts. A third was passed at 1430 A/18 and an upturned lifeboat at 2130 A/18.
At 2300 A/18, to the east of Tripoli, in approximate position 33°00'N, 14°00'E, an Italian hospital ship on course 090° passed 7 miles to the south.
This must have been the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which had departed Tripoli that evening for Tobruk. (16)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1017 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) of the following; HMS Utmost was to move her assigned patrol position 10 miles to the south. HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) leaves the Malta searched channel sunset 18th direct for position 36°18'N, 11°53'E.

In a signal timed 1736 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines that an aircraft had repored a large tanker and three merchant ships escorted by seven destroyers in position 38°15'N, 12°39'E, course 200°, speed 8 knots at 1615 A/18.

The 'DELTA' convoy was made up of the transports Titania (Italian, 5397 GRT, built 1918), Beppe (Italian, 4859 GRT, built 1912) and Capo Orso (Italian, 3149 GRT, built 1916), screened by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 1526-1530 B/17. The destroyers Giovanni da Verazzano and Antonio Pigafetta (escort leader) sailed from Naples at 1730 B/17 and 1905 B/17 respectively and joined the convoy at 2040 B/17.

The tanker Saturno (Italian, 5022 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Cagliari at 1535-1600 B/17 escorted by the torpedo boat Nicola Fabrizi. The destroyers Antonio da Noli and AscariSaturno. At 0655 B/18, Antonio da Noli joined the convoy and two hours later so did the Ascari.

At 1212 B/18-1235 B/18, the Titania and Saturno sections merged at 1212 B/18-1235 B/18. The torpedo boat Centauro sailed from Trapani at 1910 B/18 to relieve Fabrizi, which went on to this harbour, arriving at 2040 B/18.

The torpedo boat Sagittario had sailed from Palermo at 2015 B/17 to do an A/S sweep off Pantelleria. At 0028 B/19, she was ordered by Marina Messina to join the convoy off Pantelleria, to replace the torpedo boat Centauro. She met the convoy at 0923 B/19 and took position astern after which the Centauro left the convoy.

At 1250 B/19, Beppe and Giovanni da Verazzano were torpedoed and sunk by HMS P 37. At the time of the attack, the convoy was formed of Beppe, Capo Orso, Titania and Saturno, screened by Vincenzo Gioberti, Antonio da Noli, Antonio Pigafetta, Alfredo Oriani, Giovanni da Verazzano, Ascari and Sagittario. Oriani and Gioberti were ordered to hunt the submarine. They dropped depth charges and then picked up the survivors. The two destroyers proceeded to Lampedusa where they transferred the survivors to a fishing vessel which came out of the port; they then rejoined the convoy at 2006 B/19.

At 1610 A/19, HMS P 42 attacked the convoy with four torpedoes but no hits were obtained. Following this failed attack, HMS P 42 was depth charged and sustained damage which eventually forced her to abandon her war patrol.

At 2330 B/19, Titania was hit by a torpedo bomber in position 34°45'N, 12°31'E. Alfredo Oriani, Ascari and Sagittario were detached to assist the stricken vessel. Sagittario took some seriously wounded on board and brought them to Trapani at 0725 B/20. Titania was finished off by HMS P 211 at 0720 B/20. The two destroyers picked up the survivors and were ordered to land them at Tripoli if their autonomy allowed it or turn back to Trapani. Alfredo Oriani continued to Tripoli, which was reached at 1436 B/20. Ascari turned back and reached Trapani at 2251 B/20.
At 0230 B/20, the torpedo boat Circe departed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy.
Saturno, Capo Orso, Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Vincenzo Gioberti and Circe arrived at Tripoli at 1206 B/20-1420 B/20.

In a signal timed 2349 A/18, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the five submarines listed above; After passage of convoy on 19th submariens are to proceed as follows to arrive end of searched channel 1031 A on dates given: P 211 direct to arrive 20/10. Utmost through positions 35°46'N, 12°56'E and 35°40'N, 14°06'E and P 37 direct to position 35°32'N, 14°12'E both to arrive 21/10. P 42 and P 44 to spend daylight hours 20/10 vicinity Kuriat and position 35°24'N, 12°24'E respectively leaving as requisite, P 42 north of Lampedusa to position 35°41'N, 14°05'E and P 44 direct to position 35°31'N, 14°11'E to arrive 22/10. No submarine encountered is to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. (27)

19 Oct 1942 At 0239 A/19, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2349 A/18 [see one of the events for 18 October for the text of this signal]. Also received an enemy report of four ships 30 miles to the north of Pantelleria.
At 0520 A/19, HMS P 44 dived but still to the east of the ordered position.
At 1250 A/19, the enemy report from HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) timed 0840 A/19 was received.

At 1400 A/19, P 44 commenced to patrol on course 343° from position 33°43'N, 13°06'E. Lt. Barlow decided to patrol towards the convoy along their expected course until 0200 A/20 (moonset) by which time the convoy should have been encountered if making 9 knots or over. Furthermore, if the enemy had been slowed down by air or submarine attack, P 44 would be in a good position to locate them. If nothing was sighted by 0200 A/20 it was intended to proceed to the south and wait for the convoy to arrive during daylight.

At 1914 A/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1817 A/19 was received which contained the following information and orders for P 44; Aircraft report one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 35°28'N, 12°16'E, course 075° at 1540 A/19. At the same time two merchant ships and four destroyers were reported in position 35°26'N, 12°28'E, course 190°, speed 10 knots. P 44 endeavour to shadow southbound ships which torpedo aircraft will attack during night.
At 2210 A/19, distant flares were sighted ahead.
At 2225 A/19, aircraft reports were received which coincided with the position in which the flares were sighted.

At 2247 A/19, HMS P 42's (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) contact report timed 1844 A/19 was received. From this enemy report and the sighting of flares it was considered fairly certain that the convoy was steering a course of 165° instead of the reported 180°. (26)

19 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0210 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Convoy in position 37°24'N, 11°53'E, course 180°, speed 8 knots at 0058 A/19.

In a signal timed 1130 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to the above mentioned submarines; My 0210 A/19. Convoy, composition unknown, course 180° in position 310° - Lampedusa - 35 miles. (27)

20 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked an escorted ship about 80 nautical miles north-north-west of Tripoli, Libya. Three torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. This was the Italian tanker Saturno (5029 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli and Antonio Pigafetta. Da Noli sighted the torpedo tracks and the submerging submarine and dropped a few depth charges. She cruised the area for four hours and believed the submarine to have been damaged.
[For more on the convoy Saturno had been part of, see the event for 18 October 1942.]
0020 A/20 - Sighted a long trail of smoke bearing 285°. This was subsequently estimated to be 12 miles away.

0053 A/20 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0033 A/20 which contained the following information; At 2210 A/19, one merchant ship stopped in position 34°37'N, 12°34'E. One tanker and one merchant ship proceeding on course 178° at 10 knots.

0128 A/20 - P 44 suddenly broke through the smokescreen that apparently had been laid by the Italians and sighted a ship at a range of 2.5 nautical miles. Two destroyers were also seen to be in the area. One was between P 44 and the target and only about a mile away. It was decided to attack at once on the surface despite the long range. Lt. Barlow obviously thought he could be detected by the closest destroyer at any time.
0131 A/20 - In position 34°03'N, 12°35'E fired three torpedoes.

0132 A/20 - Dived. Two explosions were heard 3 minutes and 15 seconds and 3 minutes and 25 seconds after firing the first torpedo. These are thought to be hits but the results were not observed. Both destroyer started a hunt using hydrophone methods and after an hour Asdic transmissions were heard for the first time. Although both destroyers could be heard the never gained contact or got really close.
0337 A/20 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw flares bearing 210° and at the same time heard two series of explosions.
0354 A/20 - Surfaced as destroyer HE had faded out to the southward.
0410 A/20 - Dived for a destroyer approaching from astern. Hunted, inefficiently, by two destroyers.
0445 A/20 - Surfaced again and decided to investigate the ship that had been reported to be stopped at 2210 A/19.
0453 A/20 - Again put down by an approaching destroyer and hunted till daybreak. One pattern of depth charges was dropped fairly close at 0506 A/20.
0600 A/20 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Lt. Barlow decided to patrol to the northward along the convoy's route in the hope of encountering stragglers or damaged ships.
0620 A/20 - Heard a distant explosion.
0832 A/20 - Heard another distant explosion.
0856 A/20 - Heard HE bearing 095°. Sighted a destroyer proceeding northwards. Range was about 5 miles.
0912 A/20 - The destroyer was seen to disappear on a course of 160° at 26 knots after it had turned round earlier.

1610 A/20 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1416 A/20 which contained the following information and orders for P 44; Aircraft reported sighting only one merchant Ship and three destroyers, 340° - Tripoli - 25 miles at 1030 A/20. P 44 was to remain in her present position for the moment.

1904 A/20 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1746 A/20 which contained the following orders; P.44 return through position 35°31'N, 14°13'E, to arrive at the rendezvous with the minesweeper at the end of the searched channel at 1016 A/22.

2155 A/20 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0748 A/20 which had been missed in an earlier W/T routine. It had contained the following information / orders; At 0330 A/20 tanker torpedoed and merchant ship successfully bombed in position 340° - Tripoli - 76 miles. If not in contact endeavour to place yourself between the enemy and Tripoli. Air reconnaissance report will follow about noon. (16)

21 Oct 1942 At 1515 A/21, to the south of Lampedusa, in approximate position 35°20'N, 12°35'E, P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted two Baleno-class destroyers bearing 105° at a range of 5 miles. They were proceeding northwards at 24 knots.
At 1745 A/21, HMS P 44 left patrol to proceed to Malta.

At 2357 A/21, a submarine was sighted bearing 010° at a range of 3 miles which was subsequently identified as an S-class submarine. The other submarine challenged and identified herself as HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN). P 44 also challenged but apparently it was unreadable. After using S.S./T. to exchange the names of the Commanding Officers as an additional check both submarines continued on their way. (16)
22 Oct 1942 At 1408 A/22, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (also 7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined around 1030 A/22. (16)
23 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (28)
30 Oct 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) is undocked. (28)

2 Nov 1942 At 1000 A/2, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (also 8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily to form part of a line of submarines to provide cover during the Allied landings in North Africa (Operation Torch).

She departed Malta together with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN). They were escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) until around 1730 A/2 when the submarines parted company with the escort in position 36°05'N, 14°01'E.
[No log is available for this patrol so no map can be displayed.] (16)
3 Nov 1942 At 0230 A/3, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received HMS P 35's (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) signal, timed 0215 A/3, that she had successfully passed through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 0515 A/3, HMS P 44 dived and commenced her submerged passage through Q.B.B. 255.
At 1606 A/3, HE was heard on bearing 305°. It was thought to be from a petrol engine and eventually faded out to the southward.
At 1725 A/3, fast HE was heard bearing 005°. It passed astern and faded out to the southward. Both vessels proceeded down the normal searched channel towards Pantelleria.
At 2035 A/3, P 44 surfaced 12 miles to the south of Marettimo and set course for her ordered position on the patrol line. (16)

4 Nov 1942 Between 0054 A/4 and 0216 A/4, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) heard HE to the eastward. This was apparently a diesel engine from what is thought to be an A/S vessel that was carrying out hydrophone sweeps.
At 0544 A/4, P 44 dived to the north of Marettimo and made a detour to the eastward to investigate the approaches to Trapani.

At 0810 A/4, a Confienza-class torpedo boat was sighted steering 045° at 20 knots. During the day smoke indicated that a permanent A/S patrol was carried out to the westward of Trapani as far as Marettimo.
At 1830 A/4, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 38°20'N, 12°32'E which was the second billet in the patrol line extending from Cape San Vito. (16)

5 Nov 1942 At 0248 A/5, in position 293° - Cape San Vito - 15 miles, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) heard HE bearing 155°. A convoy of two transports and two destroyers was subsequently sighted at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 240° at 10 knots. P 44 increased speed to 10.5 knots and turned to chase them.
At 0328 A/5, the moon had risen behind the submarine and disclosed the targets as being two 3000-tons merchant vessels on a 140° track at a range of 3.5 miles.

At 0348 A/5, the range was still 3 miles and Lt. Barlow decided to break off the attack as the expenditure of Mk. VIII torpedoes on this target at that range was unjustifiable so early in the operation. Nor was it desired to compromise the patrol line for such a small convoy.
At 0700 A/5, P 44 commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 315° - Cape San Vito - 14 miles.
Nothing was sighted during the remainder of the day and also on the 6th and 7th. (16)

8 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked the damaged Italian light cruiser Attilio Regolo with two torpedoes of Capo San Vito, Sicily, Italy in position 38°15'N, 12°47'E. The torpedoes missed their target.

The Attilio Regolo had lost her bow due to an attack earlier that day by HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN). Attilio Regolo had laid a minefield to the east of Cape Bon together with the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Nicolo Zeno, Ascari, Mitragliere and Corazziere.

After being torpedoed by P 46 the cruiser was taken in tow by the tugs Monfalcone, Maurizio, Liguria and Trieste. A fifth tug, Poliferno joined from Palermo at 1730 A/8. They were now escorted by the destroyers Pigafetta, da Noli and Zeno as Ascari, Mitragliere and Corazziere had left them to return to Messina. The escort had been reinforced by the Italian torpedo boats Cigno and Giuseppe Cesare Abba. (Torpedo boat Lince arriving from Palermo was in sight but had not yet joined the formation while Abba may have stayed behind on an A/S search) and MAS 544 and MAS 549. Regolo reported the torpedo tracks and the destroyers Zeno and da Noli were detached to hunt the submarine but made no contact.
0957 A/8 - In position 313° - Cape San Vito - 16 miles sighted smoke on bearing 180°. Altered course to investigate.
1022 A/8 - Heard HE bearing 138°.
1025 A/8 - Heard a loud explosion and saw a column of water and an enormous cloud of smoke bearing 110°. Closed to investigate.
1200 A/8 - Nothing could be seen yet due to a lot of smoke.
1356 A/8 - Sighted a cruiser bearing 126° at a range of 6 nautical miles. It was thought she was proceeding south. Went deep to close at speed.
