1424 A/8 - Returned to periscope depth. It was seen that the cruiser was not proceeding to the south but on a course of 070°. She was being towed stern first as her bow was badly damaged. P 44 was now in a bad position to attack.

1545 A/8 - Range had now been closed to 3.5 nautical miles. The cruiser was nearly bows on with a large number of destroyers and torpedo-boats circling round her. Decided to fire two torpedoes as it seemed unlikely to close any further without being detected.
1549 A/8 - In position 38°15'N, 12°47'E fired two torpedoes.
1556 A/8 - Heard a torpedo explosion giving a running range of 8200 yards.
1558 A/8 - Heard another torpedo explosion. It is thought that the first torpedo explosion might have been a hit.
1645 A/8 - Returned to periscope depth. The cruiser was not seen.

1906 A/8 - Surfaced and passed an enemy report, timed 1757 A/8 [or 1758 A/8, sources vary]. It contained the following information for the Capt. S.10; At 1545 A/8, damaged cruiser and destroyer in position 38° 14'N, 12°49'E, course 080°, speed 3 knots.

2008 A/8 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 153A/8 which contained the following orders; P 46 has torpedoed cruiser and withdrawn, close her position with despatch. Enemy may be stopped in the vicinity.

Also the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1909 A/8 was received in which HMS P 44 and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) were given freedom of action. It also started that the enemy cruiser was still afloat at 1700 A/8. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.]
This thus seems to indicate that the cruiser had not been hit by P 44 but that perhaps a destroyer or tug had been hit instead.
2115 A/8 - Three vessels were seen approaching on the starboard quarter which we identified as torpedo boats at a range of 2 miles. One of them turned towards and increased speed.
2118 A/8 - Dived and subsequently heard the vessel pass at close range.
2152 A/8 - Surfaced and continued to proceed eastwards.
2205 A/8 - Sighted a small ship on the starboard quarter at a range of 2 miles.
2216 A/8 - Sighted another vessel approaching on the starboard beam at a range of 1 mile.
2218 A/8- Dived.

2254 A/8 - Surfaced after the HE and Asdic transmissions had ceased. There now seemed little chance to catch up with the cruisers as the area was obviously heavily patrolled by the enemy. Still held on to the eastward until 0200 A/9 when Cape Gallo was seen to be 4 miles away but these was still no sign of the cruiser. (29)
9 Nov 1942 At 0205 A/9, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) turned round to return to her patrol billet.
At 0257 A/9, three distant explosions were heard.
At 0330 A/9, a vessel was seen proceeding on a parallel course about 3 miles to the southward.
At 0421 A/9, a small vessels was seen to round Cape San Vito proceeding westwards. Both vessel seen are thought to be patrol craft which passed each other around 0425 A/9.
At 0430 A/9, the westbound vessel was illuminated by a searchlight and was seen to be an armed trawler of about 300 tons.

At 0527 A/9, HMS P 44 dived for the day and commenced to patrol off Cape San Vito. For most of the day P 44 patrolled at 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up, this due to the bad weather conditions which made periscope depth patrol impossible.

At 2017 A/9, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0914 A/9 was received. Return to position 'F'. At 1400 A/9, the line 'F-J' is to move at economical speed 225° - 8 miles. When in new position 'F', P 44 is not to cross the 100 fathom line except when in pursuit of the enemy.
In this new patrol position, P 44 however was forced to keep to the seaward owing to repeated enemy searchlight operations from shore, particularly those at Cape Ferro. (29)
10 Nov 1942 At 0530 A/10, when 7.5 miles to the west of Cape San Vito, and just before diving, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted two torpedo boats bearing 180° at a range of 2 miles.
During the day large numbers of enemy aircraft, mostly transport planes and bombers were seen flying to the southwest at low altitude.
At 2111 A/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0832 A/10 was received in which patrol line 'F-J' was to return to it's original position at economical speed as from 1400 A/10. (29)

11 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked a merchant vessel with two torpedoes off Cape San Vito in position 38°12'N, 12°46'E. Both torpedoes missed. This was the Italian transport Cerere (1198 GRT, built 1920) which observed the two torpedo tracks.

0329 A/11 - In position 320° - Cape San Vito - 5 miles, sighted two ships steering north-east just clearing the land. At first sight these were thought to be a submarine with an motor torpedo boat escort at a range of 2 miles. As this 'submarine' was escorted it was assumed she was hostile and an attack was started.
0335 A/11 - Torpedo fire had been held to make sure of the identity of the target and as she now had turned towards it was thought she was diving.

0336 A/11 - Dived. The HE that was picked up showed that both vessels were still on the surface. Also it became apparent that neither was diesel propelled. It was now thought that the target may have been a small torpedo boat. HE eventually faded out to the south.
0358 A/11 - Surfaced. Both vessels were just visible against the land proceeding southwards. It is thought their turn towards was just a navigational alteration of course.
0523 A/11 - Just before diving, two vessels were sighted to the south-east.

0545 A/11 - Identified the vessels as a 4000-tons merchant vessel in ballast escorted by a torpedo boat. Enemy course was 015° apparently bound for Naples. No attack was made on this empty enemy vessel.

0815 A/11 - Three RD-class minesweepers appeared from the direction of Trapani up the inshore swept channel and then proceeded to sweep the area to the north of Cape San Vito inside the 100 fathom line until 1300 A/11.
1027 A/11 - Three small steamers were seen approaching from the east along the searched channel. These were thought to be the tugs which had assisted the damaged cruiser now returning to Trapani.
1215 A/11 - Sighted an RD-class minesweeper towing a double Oropesa sweep along the searched channel in the direction of Cape Gallo.
1258 A/11 - Sighted smoke bearing 105°. Soon afterwards sighted that this was coming from a 2000 tons merchant vessel steering a course of 265°. Range was 5 nautical miles. Started attack.
1349 A/11 - In position 38°12'N, 12°46'E fired two torpedoes from 900 yards. Both missed.
1430 A/11 - Heard HE of motor torpedo boats investigating the area of the attack.
1645 A/11 - Sighted two motor torpedo boats off Cape San Vito.
1829 A/11 - Surfaced.

1850 A/11 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1754 A/11 in which HMS P 44 was ordered to leave patrol and return to Malta through positions 'A' (38°00'N, 11°44'E), 'B' (37°48'N, 11°44'E) at 0530 A/12. (30)

12 Nov 1942 At 0100 A/12, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2359 A/11 which ordered her to arrive at Malta at 1201 A/13 by proceeding on the surface on the 13th. Bombing restictions have been arranged.
At 0530 A/12, P 44 dived to pass through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) on the ordered route.
At 1830 A/12, P 44 surfaced 10 miles to the south of Granitola light and proceeded at speed clear of Q.B.B. 255. At 2215 A/12 course was changed for Malta. (29)

13 Nov 1942 Around 1415 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean), and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (also 8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
At 1100 A/13, HMS Una arrived off the end of the searched channel and made contact with HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN).
At 1250 A/13, HMS P 44 also joined and the submarines proceeded towards the harbour escorted by HMS Hythe. (31)

18 Nov 1942 At 1130 A/18, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (also 9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Buerat south off 32°00'N, west of 16°00'E. She was to patrol close off Buerat at dawn every day as it was suspected enemy submarines proceeded there with supplies.
She was escorted out until 1345 A/18 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR).
19 Nov 1942 At 0350 A/19, in position 33°56'N, 15°08'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted a hospital ship which passed 3 miles ahead on a course of 045°.
This was most likely the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) on passage from Tripoli to Naples.

At 0830 A/19, P 44 surfaced to make better speed to the southward but at 1036 A/19, she was forced to dive when an Italian S.79 aircraft was sighted 3 miles away coming out of a rain quall. Ten minutes later a second one was sighted an Lt. Barlow decided to remain dived. (16)
21 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked the Italian submarine Santorre di Santarosa with four torpedoes off Buerat. All torpedoes missed.
Later the same day P 44 engaged shipping inside the harbour with the deck gun damaging the Italian sailing vessel Littoria (129 GRT, built 1920).

0727 A/21 - In position 31°27'N, 15°45'E sighted up sun, the conning tower of a submarine baring 090°. Enemy course was 265°. Range was 6000 yards. Enemy speed appeared to be very high. Started attack.
0737 A/21 - Fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. All missed and most likely the torpedoes ran aground on a sand bank.

0940 A/21 - While searching for the submarine P 44 grounded on an uncharted shoal. Came up to 10 feet and retired to seaward. No fire came from the shore though despite the fact that P 44 was visible for 5 minutes.
1220 A/21 - Sighted an aircraft which carried out an A/S search for about an hour. Around 1235 A/21, the aircraft had dropped a bomb a long way off.

1340 A/21 - Sighted the enemy submarine leaving the harbour. She could not be intercepted as P 44 was too far to the west. Most likely the approach from the west of this morning of this submarine was due to a bad landfall.
1410 A/21 - A small schooner had left harbour at the same time as the submarine and was now 2 miles to the westwards steering north. P 44 closed to attack her.

1425 A/21 - After closing at full speed for 6 minutes it was seen that the schooner had altered course to the south-west and was now 3000 yards away proceeding back to the harbour. It was not possible to intercept her wile submerged before the water became too shallow.

1745 A/21 - Surfaced and closed the harbour entrance. Three or four small ships were sighted in the harbour in the forenoon. Decided for gun action in the hope of sinking some of these and if possible the pier before the reply would come too hot.

1803 A/21 - Opened fire on a 150 ton schooner, the largest vessel visible from a range of 1000 yards. Scored ten to twelve hits out of sixteen rounds fired. Then shifted target to the pier and a small steamer alongside it. After firing twelve rounds for six hits some small arms fire opened up from the beach, followed by light and erratic field gun fire. At the same time the gun malfunctioned.
1807 A/21 - Broke off the action and retired to the seaward. The schooner was seen to be smoke and to be lower in the water. She may have settled on the bottom.

1845 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1400 A/21 which contained the following orders for P 44; Leave current patrol area at 1800 A/22 by coastal route to Khoms, thence through positions 33°30'N, 14°15'E and 33°30'N, 12°50'E to establish patrol off Kerkennah by dawn/25. You are to gain time where necessary by surface passage by day. No northbound ship from Tripoli is to be attacked. In view of moon condition submarines on coastal route must keep outside visibility distance from the shore. (32)

22 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) patrolled to the east of Buerat. There was much air activity along the coast as on the previous days but motor transport on the roads was seen to be much less as compared to the two previous days.

At 1745 A/22, just before surfacing HE was picked up on bearing 250° and a small vessel was seen in the moonlight. This was subsequently identified as a Spica-class torpedo boat conducting an A/S patrol.
At 1833 A/22, P 44 surfaced after the torpedo boat had been lost from sight. Course was set for the northward in accordance with the Capt. S.10's orders in his signal timed 1400 A/21. (16)

23 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked a convoy of small vessels to the north of Misurata. The attack had to be broken off shortly after it started when P 44's gun jammed and enemy opposition was fierce.

The convoy was made up of the German motor minesweeper R 15, the German landing vessels F 545, F 546, F 360, a Siebel ferry and two L-boats (apparently the F-boats were towing the smaller vessels). These vessels were en-route to Tripoli from Ras el-Aáli [in approximately 30°22'60"N, 18°48'00"E] where they had departed around 1720 A/18.

At 2115 A/18, F 546 stopped when the tow connection between her and the Siebel ferry broke. Problems were encountered re-establishing it and eventually the convoy anchored of Ras Lanuf around 2400 A/18.
The passage to Tripoli was continued at 0730 A/20 but in the afternoon both F-boats had difficulties with their tows when the line broke on the both of them and needed to be re-established.
Around 0040 A/22, the convoy anchored of Buerat.
Around 1500 A/22, the convoy left Buerat to continue the passage. The landing vessel F 360 had joined the convoy.
At 0255 A/23, the convoy was sighted by an enemy aircraft.

At 0540 A/23, R 15 sighted a vessel coming towards which was soon after seen to be a submarine. The submarine was closing the last vessel of the convoy which was F 360. R 15 changed course to engage the enemy with all guns. The enemy submarine then opened fire on F 360 which now also opened fire on the submarine after which the submarine dived. R 15 passed over the diving spot and dropped a few depth charges following the attack.
At 2150 A/23, the convoy anchored off Tajura
At 0600 A/24, the convoy got underway again and entered Tripoli harbour at 1045 A/24.
0447 A/23 - Received an enemy report (timed 0430 A/23) from an aircraft of five vessel in the Gulf of Sirte in position 32°13'N, 15°24'E.
Lt. Barlow presumed these to be proceeding westwards.
0521 A/23 - While north of Misurata in position 32°31'N, 15°07'E sighted six ships to the southward. Closed to engage with the gun as they were too small to use torpedoes.

0544 A/23 - Opened fire from 800 yards at the rear vessel thought to be a lighter in tow of a tug. At the same moment, the leading vessel come into the moon and was seen to be a German motor torpedo boat. Both the motor torpedo boat and the lighter, now seen to be a self propelled barge, opened a hot fire. At this moment the gun jammed. P 44 had to break of the action and dived.

0546 A/23 - After diving five depth charges were dropped, all fairly close. It was now thought that the convoy was made up of one motor torpedo boat, three self propelled barges and two or three small steamers. The hunt was short and no more depth charges were dropped.
0801 A/23 - Surfaced to secure the deck gun and recover the Lewis guns.
0804 A/23 - Dived and continued westwards while keeping 3 miles from the shore.

1616 A/23 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1426 A/23 [another source gives the time of origin of this signal as 1416 A/23] which contained the following orders; My 1400 A/21. Proceed with despatch by route given to position 33°30'N, 12°50'E thence to patrol in the vicinity of position 32°52'N, 12°52'E. Merchant ships leaving Tripoli are not to be attacked.
1635 A/23 - Surfaced and proceeded westwards through the ordered positions. (33)
24 Nov 1942 At 0600 A/24, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), having encountered heavy rain squalls and low cloud, continued on the surface to reach the new patrol position with all despatch.
At 0830 A/24, P 44 dived as it was not possible to keep an effective lookout in the prevailing weather conditions.
Between 0935 A/24 and 1325 A/24, distant bomb explosions were heard.
At 1326 A/24, P 44 surfaced and continued the passage towards the ordered patrol position at 10 knots.

At 1347 A/24, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1305 A/24 was received which contained the following; You were probably sighted at 0930 A/24. At 0939 A/24 aircraft reported one merchant ship stationary, 1 miles from Zuara.
At 1406 A/24, smoke was sighted bearing 200°.
At 1416 A/24, P 44 had to dive for two aircraft flying eastwards. At that moment no smoke had been visible.
At 1437 A/24, P 44 surfaced and continued the chase. It was believed smoke was sighted bearing 160° but a rain squall reduced visibility.
At 1442 A/24, P 44 had to dive again for an approaching aircraft.
At 1515 A/24, The smoke was seen for the last time bearing 115° in the still persistent rain squalls.
At 1730 A/24, P 44 surfaced 5 miles from the shore and patrolled to the westwards keeping 3 miles from the shore.
At 2115 A/24, in position 32°55'N, 12°25'E, a vessel was seen bearing 235° at a range of 5 miles. It was thought this may be the vessel that was reported earlier in the day being stopped off Zuara.

By 2200 A/24, it had not been possible to positively identify the vessel, which was stopped, unescorted, and apparently of small size. Lt. Barlow decided to lay off till daylight before expending torpedoes or ammunition. (16)

25 Nov 1942 At 0030 A/25, in position 32°52'N, 12°25'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted four small vessels proceeding westwards about 2 miles from the shore. These were not attack as per the instructions of the Capt. S.10 not to attack enemy shipping leaving Tripoli.
At 0600 A/25, P 44 dived and closed the mysterious vessel. This was subsequently seen to be a wreck of ancient vintage. P 44 then patrolled eastwards along the coast.
At 0840 A/25, a mast was sighted bearing 238°. Closed to attack.
At 0855 A/25, the mast was seen to be of a schooner of about 100 tons which was proceeding eastwards keeping 1.5 miles from the shore.

At 0920 A/25, the attack was broken off after the range had been closed to 2000 yards. Depth of the water was now only 9 fathoms. Gun action was not possible due to the fighter escort of the schooner. Expenditure of a torpedo on this vessel was considered unjustified.

At 2005 A/25, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1851 A/25 was received which ordered P 44 to patrol on the Tripoli - Kerkennah No.8 buoy to intercept a westbound merchant vessel. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.]

At 2109 A/25, two black shapes were sighted against the land to the southward. It was thought this may be the vessel meant in the signal received an hour earlier and an escort. They were however repeatedly lost in cloud and it was eventually decided they were too slow and going in the wrong direction.
At 2215 A/25, P 44 continued her passage to the north-west at 10.5 knots. (16)
26 Nov 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) attacked an escorted transport with two torpedoes about 50 nautical miles north-west of Tripoli, Libya. No hits were obtained.

This was most probably the motor vessel Brindisi (Italian, 1976 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Animoso and Giacomo Medici. They had sailed from Tripoli the previous evening for Sfax, Sousse and eventually Trapani. At 0230 A/26, Brindisi had an accidental fire in No.1 boiler room but it was quickly extinguished and does not appear to have been related to P 44's attack which was unobserved.
0112 A/26 - In position 33°24'N, 12°33'E sighted three ships bearing 105°. Range was 6 nautical miles.
0125 A/26 - The targets were identified as a medium seized merchant vessel escorted by two torpedo boats. Enemy course was estimated to be 315°, range 2.5 nautical miles. Closed at full speed.
0150 A/26 - Fired two torpedoes from 5000 yards. After 1,5 minutes a faint explosion was heard which was thought to be one of the torpedoes exploding on hitting the bottom in 75 fathoms.
0211 A/26 - Surfaced and chased the enemy but his speed was to great.
0255 A/26 - Lost touch. Set course to patrol to the south-east.
0600 A/26 - Patrolled on the line ordered (on the route between Tripoli and the Kerkennah No.8 buoy).
1830 A/26 - Set course to patrol near Kerkennah No.8 buoy.

2359 A/26 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2330 A/26 which ordered HMS P 44 to patrol off the Kerkennah No.4 buoy. She was also ordered to attack only southbound traffic. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal].
Nothing was however sighted the following day. (16)

28 Nov 1942 At 0150 A/28, in the Kerkennah bank area, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) sighted a 4000-tons merchant vessel escorted by a torpedo boat on bearing 130° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 350°. In according with the orders this northbound vessel was not attacked.
[Possibly Foscolo (Italian, 4538 GRT, built 1942) escorted by Fortunale on passage from Tripoli to Palermo ?]
At 0610 A/28, P 44 proceeded inshore towards the Kerkennah No.4 buoy.
At 1740 A/28, P 44 surfaced and patrolled in the vicinity of Kerkennah no.4 Buoy.

At 1940 A/28, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1741 A/28 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol after dark tonight 28th November via Kerkennah. Usual rendezvous at 1135 A/30. (32)
29 Nov 1942 At 0500 A/29, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) left patrol to proceed to Malta as instructed in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1741 A/28. (16)
30 Nov 1942 In the afternoon, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (also 9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1130 A/30. (34)
8 Dec 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for 24 hours of exercises near Malta. She was swept out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). (35)
9 Dec 1942 Early in the afternoon, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) returned to Malta from exercises near Malta.
She, and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) which was returning from patrol, were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 1100 A/8. (35)

11 Dec 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (also 10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Tunis Bay off the Skerki Bank in the vicinity of position 37°32'N, 10°38'E.
She was escorted out by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which parted company around 1500 A/11. (16)
11 Dec 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 44 during her 10th war patrol see the map below.

12 Dec 1942 At 0102 A/12, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1950 A/12 in which she was ordered to patrol in the entrance to the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°09'N, 10°33'E instead of the earlier ordered position.
At 0605 A/12, P 44 dived in position 36°22'N, 11°54'E and course was set to proceed to position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E).
At 1335 A/12, HMS P 44 commenced an attack on a small merchant vessel sighted in position 36°48'N, 11°47'E. Enemy course was 120°, speed 9 knots.
At 1353 A/12, the attack was broken off when the vessel was seen to be a small Italian hospital ship of about 1500 tons.
At 1400 A/12, the hospital ship altered course to 080° and proceeded towards Pantelleria.
At 1900 A/12, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 37°04'N, 11°39'E. Course was set to proceed to position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E).
At 2330 A/12, HMS P 44 was clear of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area). Course was set to proceed to the ordered patrol position. (36)
13 Dec 1942 At 0600 A/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position (37°09'N, 10°33'E).
At 1857 A/13, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 37°09'N, 10°35'E. She continued to patrol in the vicinity of the ordered position with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 2038 A/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1744 A/13 was received which ordered the submarines on patrol to be as accurately in their patrol positions as possible by dawn/14 for the interception of approaching convoys. (16)
14 Dec 1942 At 0005 A/14, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat bearing 080° which was closing rapidly.
At 0020 A/14, P 44 surfaced and continued patrol.
At 0614 A/14, P 44 dived in position 37°10'N, 10°36'E and patrolled within two miles of the ordered patrol position all day.
At 0920 A/14, the masts of a schooner were sighted at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 240°.
[possibly Esmeralda ???]
At 0930 A/14, distant depth charging was heard to the northward.
At 1104 A/14, further explosions were heard to the northward.
At 1430 A/14, distant heavy depth charging was heard. [This was most likely the depth charging of HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN)]
At 1715 A/14, four motor torpedo boats passed within half a mile. The were in close order, line ahead proceeding on course 330° at 15 knots.
This must have been the German S 33, S 57, S 58 and S 61 which were on passage from Sousse (departed at 1225 A/14) to Bizerta (arrived at 1830 A/14).
At 1820 A/14, P 44 surfaced in position 37°09'N, 10°39'E.
At 1845 A/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0800 A/14 was received in which P 44 was ordered once more to patrol in the ordered position as accurately as possible.
At 1913 A/14, a northbound ship was sighted bearing 225° at a range of 4 miles.
At 2008 A/14, an attack was commenced. On closing the enemy was seen to be an unescorted 2500 tons merchant vessel in ballast. Enemy course was 045°, speed 6 knots.
[Possibly Achille Lauro ???].

In view of the expected southbound convoy and the patrolling motor torpedo boats known to be in the vicinity, this vessel was considered not important enough to attack and thus compromise the submarines position.
At 2200 A/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2145 A/14 was received which warned about the operations of Allied motor torpedo boats between Plane Island and Marettimo. (37)
15 Dec 1942 At 0554 A/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 37°13'N, 10°39'E.
At 0645 A/15, whilst at 70 feet the HE of three destroyers was picked up which subsequently passed overhead at 22 knots (as per revolutions).
At 0653 A/15, P 44 was at periscope depth. Three destroyers were seen hull down to the southward bound for Tunis.

At 0724 A/15, two large destroyers were seen to the west-south-west at a range of 5 miles. P 44 closed at speed. One of the destroyers was thought to be a Navigatori-class and one an Avieri (Soldati)-class.

At 0734 A/15, it was seen that these two destroyers were escorting two transports whose masts and funnels could just be seen over the horizon. P 44 appeared to be 60° to 70° on the port bow of these transports who were steering a steady course in line abreast with the two destroyers zigzagging ahead. Course of the convoy was about 200°, speed 10 to 12 knots. P 44 continued to close at speed but it was not possible to close enough to attack and the convoy passed well out of range.
At 1124 A/15, smoke was sighted to the southward at an estimated range of about 10 miles. Closed to investigate and attack.
At 1200 A/15, the source of the smoke was identified as two small Italian minesweepers, sweeping on a course of 020°.

At 1215 A/15, the attack was broken off. Two German landing craft were sighted half a mile away. They appeared to have come from the direction of Plane Island on a course of 130° across the Gulf of Tunis. They passed 500 yards astern. They were numbers F 1800 and F 1900. Their cargo consisted of a number of large wooden crates. Armed German soldiers were also seen on board.
This were most likely the German landing ships F 150 and F 480 on the way from Bizerta (departed around 0800 A/15) to Tripoli via Sfax.
At 1408 A/15, P 44 sighted what appeared to be three large ships to the southward. Closed to investigate and attack.
At 1420 A/15, the ships were identified as three Avieri (Soldati)-class destroyers. They were in line ahead and zigzagging on a mean course of 030° at a speed of 20 knots.
At 1425 A/15, P 44 altered course to let the destroyers pass astern.

At 1428 A/15, when the leading destroyer was abreast of the submarine she put her wheel hard over and came straight towards. P 44 went to 60 feet and shut off for depth charging. The HE however did not get neared so P 44 returned to periscope depth and saw the destroyers continuing on their original mean course. Most likely a zig had failed the Commanding Officer's nerves.

At 1537 A/15, two Navigatori-class destroyers to the southward proceeding on course 025° at 20 knots. All these destroyers were not attacked in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1043 A/15. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 15 December 1942.]
At 1740 A/15, P 44 returned to periscope depth to have a good look around in the last of daylight. Three distant explosions were heard to the northward and almost imediately smoke was sighted.
At 1745 A/15, HE was picked up to the northward and two dark shapes were seen.
At 1748 A/15, an attack was commenced on these 'dark shapes' which were thought to be a transport and an escorting destroyer.
At 1755 A/15, the HE was identified as being from two destroyers proceeding at 18 knots. The attack was broken off, course was altered away and depth was increased to 70 feet.
At 1900 A/15, P 44 surfaced in position 37°09'N, 10°41'E.
At 2007 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1825 A/15 was received which contained information on approaching convoys. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
At 2017 A/15, HE of a motor torpedo boat was picked up on bearing 210° but nothing was sighted. This HE faded at 2055 A/15.
At 2058 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1122 A/15 was received in which she was ordered to patrol in position 37°45'N, 11°10'E as of dawn/16.
At 2118 A/15, course was set to proceed to this newly ordered patrol position. (38)

15 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1043 A/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN), HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN); Reconnaissance shows success in your area. You are doing splendidly, naturally the enemy like to hunt you with all available forces though these not numerous. Enemy traffic must continue down channel where you are disposed and this is expected in immediate future. You should therefore avoid detection and endeavour to maintain position. Do not read special routines if this jeopardises security, maintain W/T silence under all circumstances. If hard pressed withdraw to north or east by safe route, returning when enemy shaken off. (39)

16 Dec 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) attacked a convoy made up of the transports Campania (Italian, 5247 GRT, built 1918) and the Rhea (German, 1388 GRT, built 1922). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Groppo and Giuseppe Sirtori. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. Rhea observed one torpedo missing her on the starboard side.
0216 A/16 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0145 A/16. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
0553 A/16 - Dived in position 37°44'N, 11°08'E and commenced to patrol in that vicinity. Distant depth charging was heard at interval throughout the entire day.
1520 A/16 - Passed through a large amount of floating wreckage in position 37°46'N, 11°05'E.
1600 A/16 - The Officer of the Watch sighted a large column of smoke and water shoot up some 200 feet into the air bearing 130° at a range of about 6 miles.

1634 A/16 - Sighted two merchant vessels escorted by a older type torpedo-boat and an escort what was thought to be a sloop like Eritrea but then smaller. The convoy was northbound and the merchant vessels were of 3500 and 2500 tons and were fairly light. Started attack.

1717 A/16 - In position 37°46'N, 11°05'E, fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards at the two merchant vessels that were overlapping. No hits were obtained. P 44 had gone to 120 feet on firing and had moved off the tracks, keeping stern on to the nearest escort vessel. Five minutes after firing a hunt started which lasted for about an hour. Only five depth charges were dropped but they were not near.
2007 A/16 - Surfaced in position 37°45'N, 11°05'E.

2100 A/16 - Dived to reload the torpedo tubes with two Mk. II and two MK. IV torpedoes set to 10 and 12 feet. [Due to a torpedo shortage of Mk. VIII torpedoes at Malta, older type of torpedoes had to be taken on board the submarines operating from Malta at the time.]
2239 A/16 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes. Surfaced about half an hour later. (16)

17 Dec 1942 At 0135 A/17, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0105 A/17 in which she was ordered to proceed to a new patrol position. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but most likely the position ordered was 37°26'N, 10°54'E.
At 0200 A/17, P 44 altered course to 215° to proceed to the newly ordered patrol position.
At 0800 A/17, P 44 dived in position 37°26'N, 10°54'E.
At 0936 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0720 A/17 was received in which P 44 was ordered to patrol in position 37°31'N, 10°59'E by 1158 A/17.
At 0951 A/17, course was set to proceed to this newly ordered patrol position.
At 1146 A/17, P 44 arrived in position 37°31'N, 10°59'E.
At 1154 A/17, distant heavy depth charging was heard.
At 1157 A/17, smoke was sighted to the north-west.

At 1205 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1012 A/17 [another source give the time of origin of this signal as 1019 A/17] was received. It contained the following information; At 0950 A/17 aircraft reported one motor vessel and two destroyers in position 38°17'N, 12°28'E, course 235°, speed 15 knots.

At 1212 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0932 A/17 was received. This signal contained the following information; At 0718 A/17 aircraft sighted one motor vessel and two destroyers in position 38°02'N, 11°43'E, course 245° speed 15 knots.
At 1216 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0955 A/17 was received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but it contained information on a convoy.]
At 1830 A/17, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 37°32'N, 11°01'E.
At 2046 A/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1920 A/17 was received which contained the following orders for P 44; Patrol north of Sicily between 38°00'N / 38°25'N and 12°03'E and 12°53'E.
At 2130 A/17, course was altered to proceed to the new patrol area.
At 2138 A/17, P 44 dived in position 37°48'N, 11°18'E for two small dark objects bearing 340° at a range of 2 to 3 miles. No HE was picked up after diving.

At 2345 A/17, P 44 surfaced and sighted six motor torpedo boats laying stopped on a north-south patrol line at about half a mile intervals. Range was about 2 to 3 miles. It is presumed these motor torpedo boats were listening for submarine activity.
At 2347 A/17, P 44 dived again to avoid being sighted. P 44 remained dived until 2347 A/18. (16)
18 Dec 1942 Between 0037 A/18 and 0115 A/18, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) heard distant depth charging.
This must have been the depth charging of HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN).
By 0321 A/18, no more HE of the motor torpedo boats sighted for the first time late last evening was heard so P 44 surfaced in position 37°53'N, 11°26'E. Nothing was in sight.

As it was now impossible to reach Cape San Vito before daylight, Lt. Roxburgh decided to proceed to the north-west of Marettimo and dive there and patrol eastwards along the expected track of the Tunisian convoys.
At 0600 A/18, P 44 dived in position 38°09'N, 11°45'E.
At 0635 A/18, HE was picked up on bearing 145° which was identified as being from a destroyer / torpedo boat.
At 0640 A/18, this HE faded. On coming to periscope depth nothing was sighted.
At 0925 A/18, distant depth charging was heard.
Between 1538 A/18 and 1617 A/18, heavy depth charging was heard. This was HMS P 212 getting another depth charge shower.
At 1830 A/18, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 38°17'N, 12°19'E.
At 1854 A/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1201 A/18 was received in which P 44 was ordered to patrol in position 38°20'N, 11°53'E by dawn/19.
At 1930 A/18, course was set to proceed to the newly ordered patrol position. (16)

19 Dec 1942 At 0612 A/19, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 38°23'N, 11°54'E. It was very difficult to keep an efficient patrol at periscope depth due to the weather conditions.
By 0730 A/19, there was no sign yet of the expected convoy. Lt. Roxburgh decided to patrol at 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 0742 A/19, HE was picked up on bearing 070°. Returned to periscope depth. While coming up the Asdic operator reported fast destroyer HE approaching on a steady bearing. Lt. Roxburgh decided to wait at 50 feet until the destroyer had passed overhead.

At 0746 A/19, P 44 was at periscope depth. One Avieri (Soldati)-class destroyer was sighted at a range of 500 yards. Also a 7000-tons modern merchant vessel was sighted at a range of 600 yards with P 44 60° on the port bow. A second similar looking merchant vessel was seen at a range of 500 yards with P 44 10° on it's starboard bow. Enemy course was 225°, speed 25 knots.
When about to open fire depth control was lost due to the weather conditions and
P 44 ended up at 35 feet.

At 0755 A/19, P 44 was back at periscope depth and the opportunity to fire to torpedoes had passed. A third merchant vessel was subsequently sighted right astern coming directly towards. P 44 went to 50 feet and by the time she returned to periscope depth there was no opportunity to fire torpedoes at it. Another Avieri (Soldati)-class destroyer was now sighted only 300 yards away. Once again P 44 went down and let the destroyer pass.
On return to periscope depth it was seen that aircraft were patrolling overhead and a third destroyer and possibly a fourth was sighted.
[Mario Roselli, Alfredo Oriani and Calino from Palermo to Bizerta ???]

At 1435 A/19, in position 38°21'N, 11°53'E, HMS P 44 sighted an unescorted merchant vessel of about 2500-tons. She was in ballast and proceeding on a course of 095° at 4 knots. This merchant vessel was towing a schooner of about 200-tons which in turn was towing something submerged. P 44 closed to 4000 yards but no attack was made on this empty northbound vessel.
At 1827 A/19, P 44 surfaced in position 38°22'N, 11°57'E.

At 1846 A/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1206 A/19 was received in which she was ordered to investigate the approaches to Trapani for enemy patrols. She was not to compromise her position there unless a really worthwhile target was sighted such as enemy battleships or cruisers. (16)
20 Dec 1942 At 0345 A/20, in position 38°26'N, 12°05'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat bearing 180° at 2.5 miles range.
At 0445 A/20, P 44 surfaced and continued her passage to the Trapani area.
At 0610 A/20, P 44 dived in position 38°17'N, 12°13'E.
At 0800 A/20, P 44 arrived in the intended patrol position off the Forcelli Rocks.
At 0840 A/20, smoke was sighted bearing 170°.
At 0917 A/20, a mast was sighted bearing 190°, range 7 miles.
At 1009 A/20, smoke was sighted bearing 090°.
At 1030 A/20, two minesweepers were sighted to the eastwards sweeping in the swept channel.

At 1100 A/20, a modern laden merchant vessel of about 5000-tons was sighted. Range was 6000 yards. This vessel was leaving Trapani harbour after which it altered course to 015° and proceeded up the channel just swept by the minesweepers. No attack was made in accordance with the orders.
At 1102 A/20, four schooners were sighted to the north-east proceeding close inshore.
At 1120 A/20, a small coastal tanker was seen proceeding close inshore on course 350° to the east of Levanzo Island.
At 1324 A/20, a small merchant vessel was seen bearing 125° proceeding on course 200°.
At 1341 A/20, smoke was seen bearing 225° at a range of 8 miles. This were presumably minesweepers proceeding towards Marettimo Island.
At 1406 A/20, HE was picked up bearing 110° but nothing was sighted.
At 1500 A/20, a small merchant vessel was sighted bearing 238° steering a southerly course.
At 1600 A/20, P 44 commenced to retire to the northward.
At 1828 A/20, P 44 surfaced in position 38°11'N, 12.20'E. (16)
21 Dec 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) attacked an enemy destroyer or torpedo-boat with two torpedoes about 25 nautical miles north of Marettimo Island. Both torpedoes missed.
The target was probably the Italian torpedo boat Cigno which was on an A/S search. The attack was not observed.
0028 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0004 A/21 which ordered P 44 to patrol in position 38°20'N, 11°53'E.

0044 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0015 A/21 in which she was ordered to attack a flotilla of Italian fleet destroyers. Also orders were included to leave patrol and return to Malta. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
0056 A/21 - Dived for two motor torpedo boats in position 38°18'N, 12°15'E.
0100 A/21 - Altered course to proceed to the new patrol position.
0140 A/21 - Started to retract some torpedoes from the tubes to set them to 8 feet.
0325 A/21 - Came to periscope depth for the 0330 hours W/T routine. Two torpedoes were now ready set to 8 feet.
0333 A/21 - Heard HE ahead on the starboard bow.
0334 A/21 - In position 38°25'N, 12°04'E sighted an Italian fleet destroyer, thought to be a Maestrale-class moving across the bow. Enemy speed was thought to be 19 knots. Started attack.

0338 A/21 - Fired the only two available torpedoes from 1500 yards. (P 44 had four torpedoes left, two Mk. II and two Mk. IV. The depth settings of these torpedoes could not be changed while they were in the tubes and as P 44 had received a signal shortly before to attack Italian destroyers the depth setting had to be changed and she was in the middle of doing so). Both torpedoes missed. P 44 had gone to 120 feet on firing. The destroyer turned to port and started a hunt. She was in contact one or twice and hunted P 44 for 4 hours, during which time not a single depth charge was dropped. It was decided she was probably a Spica-class destroyer bound for Trapani.
0652 A/21 - No more HE was heard. Returned to periscope depth. Saw the destroyer still hunting about 4 nautical miles astern.
0810 A/21 - Left patrol and set course to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0015 A/21.
1820 A/21 - Heard distant depth charging.
1844 A/21 - Surfaced in position 38°22'N, 11°47'E.
1855 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1225 A/21 which contained the following orders for P 44. Patrol in position 38°10'N, 11°50'E from 1758 A/22 to 0300 A/23.

As P 44 was very near this position Lt. Roxburgh decided to patrol in the current position so as not to compromise the new patrol position. Also the position P 44 was currently in was know to be on the enemy's convoy route. (16)

21 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1225 A/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN); Move to establish patrol in position and by time given. After attack or at given time leave patrol or proceed in execution previous orders. P 212, patrol in position 38°14'N, 12°31'E from 1402 A/22 to 2359 A/22.M P 44, patrol in position 38°10'N, 11°50'E from 1758 A/22 to 0300 A/23. P 42, patrol in position 37°54'N, 11°17'E from 2256 A/22 to 0642 A/23.

These orders were issued to intercept the Italian transport Caterina Costa (8060 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the Italian destroyers Geniere and Camicia Nera, which sailed from Naples at 1430 A/22-1513 A/22. Geniere ran aground on the Skerki Bank (Keith’s Reef) and had to turn back, arriving in Palermo around 1000 A/24 (see below). The convoy was reinforced by the destroyer Bombardiere from Bizerte at 1220 A/23 in position 37°35'N, 10°39'E. They arrived in Bizerte at 1621 A/23-1700 A/23.

At 1220 A/23, Geniere sighted the destroyers Legionario and Grecale and asked the former to take her in tow. She did so at 1334 A/23 but the cable quickly broke, and towing was abandoned. The three destroyers proceeded to Trapani in company. At 1420 A/23, Marina Trapani informed them that the destroyer Saetta and the tug Tifeo had sailed to their assistance. For some unknown reason, Tifeo never found them. At 1658 A/23, Saetta joined to relieve Legionario which then proceeded alone and reached Palermo at 2325 A/23. At 1733 A/23, Grecale left them and arrived in Trapani at 2010 A/23. At 1902 A/23, Geniere received a signal from Supermarina ordering her to make for Palermo instead of Trapani. At 0055 A/24, off Trapani, they were reinforced by the torpedo boat Clio. At 1004 A/24-1015 A/24, Geniere, Saetta and Clio entered Palermo.
These patrol positions were based on information from ULTRA (codebreaking). [See files; DEFE 3/585 pages 892, 975 and DEFE 3/790 frames 123, 127.] (39)
22 Dec 1942 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) attacked an escorted enemy merchant vessel with two torpedoes about 20 nautical miles north-west of Marettimo Island. No hits were obtained.

This was the Italian transport Etruria (2633 GRT, built 1933) with the German landing vessels F 478, F 482, F 483 and F 484. They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito and Angelo Bassini. Two hours later, the transports was sunk by an Allied air attack.
21 December 1942

2155 A/21 - Received an aircraft report timed 2145 A/21 in which reported one merchant vessel and four unknowns in position 38°21'N, 12°18'E, course 280°, speed 10 knots. From this aircraft report it looked like this convoy came straight towards P 44.

2242 A/21 - Received an aircraft reported timed 2210 A/21 which reported that the enemy had altered course to 250°. It thus looked like the convoy was going to pass near the position ordered by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 1225 A/21.
2250 A/21 - Altered course to 148° and increased speed to get to position 38°10'N, 11°50'E in order to intercept the convoy around 0030 A/22.
2326 A/21 - Sighted smoke bearing 080°.
2353 A/21 - Identified one merchant vessel escorted by one destroyer 240°.
2357 A/21 - Dived in position 38°19'N, 11°49'E. This was about 6 nautical miles ahead of the convoy. After diving the enemy's smoke could still be seen through the periscope.

22 December 1942 0015 A/22 - The merchant vessel was seen to be a medium-seized one of about 4000 tons. One destroyer was 1500 yards ahead and another destroyer was now seen to be astern. Also four motor torpedo boat were thought to be part of the screen. Enemy course was 240°, speed 7 knots.

0038 A/22 - When about to fire the last two remaining torpedoes in position from a range of 2000 yards the destroyer that was ahead of the merchant vessel suddenly turned towards and speeded up. She had detected P 44 on her Asdic. P 44 went deep and prepared to fire by Asdic. When the order fire was given this was not heard as the destroyer dropped one depth charge right overhead at the same time. The depth charge exploded very close. The two torpedoes were then fired but 14 seconds too late so they missed. Both destroyers then hunted P 44. They dropped 14 depth charges but these were not close.
0200 A/22 - Came to 70 feet.
0212 A/22 - Heard one distant depth charge explode.
0235 A/22 - Came to periscope depth. Another distant depth charge explosion was heard but there was nothing in sight.

0257 A/22 - Surfaced in position 38°19'N, 11°57'E, some 5 miles to the east of the attack. Set course for position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E) by passing position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) to the eastward by about 10 miles.
0338 A/22 - Sighted aircraft flares and tracer bullets bearing 270°. Presumed this to be an air attack on the convoy just missed by P 44.
0454 A/22 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 345° at a range of about 4 miles on a northerly course. P 44 altered course away but remained on the surface.
0552 A/22 - Dived in position 38°00'N, 11°41'E, for a motor torpedo boat bearing 315° at a range of 2.5 miles. The enemy was steering towards.
Throughout the day distant depth charging was heard from time to time.
1401 A/22 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1153 A/22. In this signal P 44 was ordered to arrive at the end of the Malta searched channel 0800 A/24.
1816 A/22 - Surfaced in position 37°42'N, 11°29'E, still 35 miles short of position 'H'.
2200 A/22 - Altered course to proceed to position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E). (16)
23 Dec 1942 At 0230 A/23, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) altered course to 169° in position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E). Proceeded past Pantelleria on the surface.
0600 A/23 - Dived in position 36°36'N, 11°45'E. Proceeded at 50 feet owing to the weather conditions which made constant periscope depth patrol impossible. Only came up for W/T routines.
0714 A/23 - Altered course to 106°, 10 miles to the north of position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E) and set course for Gozo Island.

0758 A/23 - Came to periscope depth for a W/T routine. Sighted a 2500-tons transport escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat bearing 050°, range 6500 yards, course 215°, speed 8 knots. Position was 36°32'N, 11°46'E. No enemy report was made as no submarines were in the area to intercept this transport. [P 44 was out of torpedoes.]
[possibly Anna Maria escorted by Fortunale on passage from Palermo to Sousse ???]
At 0951 A/23, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1220 A/23, in position 36°28'N, 12°08'E, the HE of a destroyer was heard to pass overhead.
At 1331 A/23, in position 36°27'N, 12°14'E, the HE of three destroyers was heard overhead.
At 1816 A/23, P 44 surfaced in position 36°21'N, 12°35'E. (16)
24 Dec 1942 At 0741 A/24, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 0731 A/24 to the Capt. S.10 informing him that she was 3 hours late.
At 1150 A/24, HMS P 44 proceeded unescorted up the swept Channel.
At 1325 A/24, HMS P 44 ended her 10th war patrol (also 10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
4 Jan 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 44 during her 11th war patrol see the map below.

4 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/4, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily to the south of 38°43'N and between longitudes 12°45'E and 13°33'E.

At the same time HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (also 11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Sicilian Channel to the south of the Skerki Bank (to patrol area west of line Keith Rock - Cape Bon and south of QBB 201).
They parted company around 1515 A/15, shortly after clearing the swept channel. The submarines were not escorted out.
The submarines were routed through position 35°45'N, 12°34'E and then through positions 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E).
From position 'H', HMS Una was to proceed to position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) and then to her patrol area. (31)
6 Jan 1943 At 0559 A/6, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day in position 37°17'N, 11°44'E.

At 1400 A/6, P 44 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1237 A/6 which contained the following orders; Move as convenient to operate in area previously allocated to HMS P 54 (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) between longitude of Marittimo and Cape San Vito and south of latitude 38°43'N. HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) may be encountered tonight between position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E) and your new area.
At 1444 A/6, course was altered to proceed to the new patrol area.

At 1702 A/6, in position 37°34'N, 11°09'E, P 44 sighted six columns of smoke and also heard the HE of six destroyers. These destroyers were proceeding to the north-east at 24 knots about 10 miles to the west.
At 1909 A/6, P 44 surfaced in position 37°38'N, 11°09'W. As enemy motor torpedo boats were known to be in the area P 44 proceeded slowly with the Asdic watch closed up.
At 2114 A/6, P 44 dived in position 37°46'N, 11°15'W for aircraft flares which were dropped right overhead. She surfaced again a little over half an hour later and continued her passage. (40)

7 Jan 1943 At 0015 A/7, in position 37°57'N, 11°24'E (about 30 nautical miles were of Marettimo Island), HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted two torpedo tracks coming towards. The first torpedo passed ahead but the second torpedo passed underneath the fore hatch after P 44 had begun to alter course away. P 44 then dived and shortly afterwards heard HE of an approaching motor torpedo boat.
[The identity of this boat has not yet been identified and the attack may also have been bogus.]
At 0032 A/7, the HE of motor torpedo boats was heard overhead. Starting and stopping.
At 0052 A/7, the HE disappeared. Lt. Roxburgh decided to remain dived.
At 0308 A/7, the HE of a motor torpedo boat was picked up. The enemy was proceeding northwards at speed.
At 0332 A/7, the HE of a destroyer and three motor torpedo boats was heard. They were proceeding northwards at a speed of about 22 knots (as per revolutions).
At 0810 A/7, the HE of motor torpedo boat was picked up. The enemy was proceeding to the south-east. Nothing could be seen through the periscope.

At 0833 A/7, in position 38°09'N, 11°51'E (north-west of Marettimo), two Spica-class torpedo boats were sighted proceeding on course 245° at a speed of 12 knots. They were sweeping on Asdic. Their course would take them directly towards the position of the torpedo attack on P 44.
At 1014 A/7, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1026 A/7, another distant explosion was heard.
At 1130 A/7, yet another distant explosion was heard.
At 1323 A/7, six distant explosions were heard. Two Cant. 506B aircraft were patrolling in the vicinity all forenoon.
At 1327 A/7, HE of an approaching motor torpedo boat was picked up.
At 1330 A/7, the motor torpedo boat was sighted through the periscope.
At 1340 A/7, P 44 went to 70 feet. She was being hunted by two motor torpedo boats which dropped sixteen depth charges in all but none were close.
At 1429 A/7, distant explosions were heard.
At 1440 A/7, five explosions were heard.
At 1500 A/7, eight more explosions were heard.
At 1532 A/7, the HE of a motor torpedo boat was picked up.
At 1546 A/7, a patrol vessel that looked similar to a motor launch was sighted.
At 1548 A/7, an ME 110 aircraft was seen to be patrolling in the vicinity. Until 1705 A/7, HMS P 44 was hunted by this aircraft and the enemy 'motor launch'.
At 1826 A/7, P 44 surfaced in position 38°24'N, 12°07'E.
At 1859 A/7, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1839 A/7 was received. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 7 January 1943.]
At 1940 A/7, HE was picked up on bearing 120° but nothing was sighted. This HE was lost at 1954 A/7.
At 2000 A/7, course was altered to the newly ordered patrol position.
At 2116 A/7, P 44 dived in position 38°33'N, 12°10'E for the HE of a rapidly closing motor torpedo boat. At 2139 A/7, P 44 surfaced and proceeded. (16)

7 Jan 1943 In signals timed 1731 A/7 and 1839 A/7, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) to establish a patrol line to the north of Ustica.
[We have not been able to find the text of these signals].

This was to try to intercept the Italian tanker Saturno (former French Massis, 5022 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Sirio which an ULTRA intercept had shown as expected to leave Naples for Bizerte at 0100 A/8 (see DEFE3/7/frame 86) and pass through position 38°28'N, 12°05'E at 2300 A/8, Actually the convoy sailed from Naples at 2330 A/7-0115 A/8, but Pallade had a variety of defects and only caught up with the tanker and Sirio at 1216 A/8. At 1555 A/8, a fire occurred on Saturno and the trio was diverted to Palermo, where they arrived at 1400 A/9. They sailed from Palermo at 1430 A/10-1445 A/10. At 1705 A/10, in position 298° - Cape Gallo – 4.2 miles, Pallade detected an echo at a range of 4000 metres. Saturno and Sirio turned hard to port to avoid it, while Pallade proceeded to attack the submarine which was moving at 3 knots. At 1714 A/10, twelve 180-kg depth charges were dropped, another pattern followed at 1730 A, with a third pattern of twelve depth charges at 1800. It appeared from the sonar that the target had broken in two. Pallade tried to recover debris but in the darkness could not find any. And at 1937 A/10 had rejoined the convoy. In the meantime, the Italian corvettes Antilope, Artemide and Gabbiano (1st Corvette Squadron, equipped with SAFAR P.600 sonar) sailed from Trapani at 1800 A/10-1820 A/10. They proceeded on A/S search ahead of the convoy. At 1000 A/11, a German aircraft dropped several bombs on a submarine and Antilope proceeded to look for it, She obtained a contact and dropped a pattern of depth charges at 1015 A/11 in position 37°26'N, 10°39'E, and another at 1115 A/11, observing a patch of oil. Artemide followed up with a pattern of depth charges at 1208 A/11 and another at 1420 A/11. The three corvettes returned to Trapani, arriving at 2330 A/11-0120 A/12. Saturno and her two torpedo boats arrived at Bizerte at 1450 A/11-1655 A/11. (41)

8 Jan 1943 At 0531 A/8, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived north-north-west of Ustica, in position 39°29'N, 12°53'E, for the HE of a destroyer bearing 340°, speed 18 knots (as per revolutions). HE was subsequently lost on bearing 075°.

At 1038 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0955 A/8 was received which gave information of a large enemy tanker and two escorts were at 0830 A/8, position 39°50'N, 13°05'E proceeding on course 180° at 10 knots.
At 1257 A/8, a report from an aircraft timed 1200 A/8 was received which gave the position, course and speed of the enemy convoy as 39°50'N, 13°05'E, 180°, 10 knots.

At 1639 A/8, smoke was sighted to the west, well below the horizon. At the same time A/S transmissions were heard coming from the same bearing. P 44 attempted to close at speed but the bearing of the smoke continued to draw ahead rapidly.
At 1659 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1605 A/8 was received. HMS P 44 continued to chase the smoke.

At 1751 A/8, P 44 surfaced in position 39°20'N, 12°59'E. P 44 surfaced before it was completely dark in order to identify the source of the smoke. A large merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers / torpedo boats was sighted bearing 220° at a range of 8 miles. P 44 increased to full speed and chased.
At 1812 A/8, the chase was abandoned as it was impossible to overtake. Actually the enemy only ran out. At 1816 A/8 course was altered to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1605 A/8.
At 2321 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2242 A/8 was received. (16)

8 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1605 A/8, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) to shift their patrol line 20 miles to the northward.
In a signal timed 2252 A/8, they were ordered to return to their original patrol areas.
[We have been unable to find the exact text of these signals.] (41)
9 Jan 1943 At 0531 A/9, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) altered course to 205° to make for the area to the north of Marettimo Island.

At 0314 A/9, to the north-north-west of Ustica, in position 39°25'N, 12°46'E, P 44 had to dive on the sudden appearance of a torpedo boat on an opposite course. Range was only about 400 yards due to the very poor visibility and rain. The torpedo boat apparently did not sighted the submarine which remained submerged for the day.

At 0758 A/9, the HE of a motor torpedo boat was heard to pass overhead. Due to the weather conditions, P 44 patrolled at 70 feet all day with the Asdic watch closed up. Constant periscope depth patrol was impossible and P 44 only came up for the W/T routines.
At 1828 A/9, P 44 surfaced in position 38°55'N, 12°31'E. (16)

10 Jan 1943 At 0400 A/10, in position 38°15'N, 12°14'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat that was sighted in the gloom of a searchlight on Cape San Vito. After diving the HE of two motor torpedo boats was picked up. This HE ceased at 0450 A/10.
Between 0515 A/10 and 0630 A/10, intermittent motor torpedo boat HE was heard overhead.

At 0651 A/10, P 44 came to periscope depth to obtain a fix which showed that the submarine had been set about 15 miles to the south-east during the last two days and now was only 5 miles from Trapani in position 38°04'N, 12°22'E.
At 0727 A/10, course was altered to 320° to proceed to position 38°15'N, 12°10'E.

At 1607 A/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1136 A/10 was received. P 44 and HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) were to form a patrol line to the north-west of Ustica by 0001 A/11. [We have not been able to find the text of this signal.] P 44 was to be in position 38°44'N, 12°20'E by 0001 A/11.
[To intercept the Roselli-convoy ??? (Naples - Bizerta).]
Between 1713 A/10 and 1802 A/10, distant heavy depth charging was heard.
At 1724 A/10, course was altered to 008° to close the newly ordered patrol position.
At 1836 A/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1545 A/10 was received which contained info that flares would be fired over an expected convoy coming from the north-east.
At 2312 A/10, two white lights were sighted on the horizon.

At 2350 A/10, these two white lights were identified as the expected Italian repatriation ships Giulio Cesare (21657 GRT, built 1921) and Duilio (Italian, 24281 GRT, built 1923). These two brilliantly lit ships passed two miles to the southward of P 44 on course 080° at 16 knots. (16)
11 Jan 1943 By 0001 A/11, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) had arrived in the ordered patrol position, 38°44'N, 12°20'E. She then patrolled on courses 030° - 210° through this position.
At 0332 A/11, an aircraft flare was sighted bearing 010° at a range of 8 to 10 miles. Also anti-aircraft fire traces bullets were seen. Altered course towards to close.

At 0357 A/11, the HE of destroyers was heard passing down the starboard side. The night was pitch dark and it was raining. Visibility was under 1000 yards. Asdic reported destroyer HE closing at 16 knots.

Nothing at all could be seen from the bridge and the Commanding Officer thought he was on the starboard bow of the convoy until the HE suddenly stopped passing down the starboard side and started going across the bow.
At 0405 A/11, an aircraft report timed 0315 A/11 was received which reported seven 'unkowns' in the vicinity.

At 0414 A/11, a fleet destroyer was sighted ahead at a range of only 700 yards. P 44 was about 70° on its port bow. A similar destroyer was sighted astern of the first one about the same distance away. It now appeared P 44 was on the port bow or port beam of the convoy on about a 90° track and almost on top of the port wing screen.

At 0415 A/11, a third destroyer was sighted ahead at a range of only 500 yards coming towards. P 44 was 20° to 30° on its port bow. The HE of a fourth destroyer was also picked up on the starboard bow.
At 0421 A/11, P 44 dived in position 38°48'N, 12°19'E to avoid being rammed. After diving the Asdic reported possible merchant vessel HE ahead but it was drowned by the HE of the destroyers.
At 0452 A/11, P 44 surfaced in position 38°49'N, 12°18'E after all HE had crossed ahead. Nothing was in sight. Set course to 210° to chase.
At 0628 A/11, P 44 dived in position 38°46'N, 12°17'E and patrolled in the vicinity of the patrol position all day.
Between 0917 A/11 and 1012 A/11, distant explosions were heard.
At 1830 A/11, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 38°47'N, 12°19'E.

At 1852 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1631 A/11 was received which contained the following orders for P 44; Patrol in the area east of 14°20'E and between latitudes 39°00'N and 40°10'E. Enter your area by the south western comer. You may spend up to 48 hours gunning trains.
At 1918 A/11, course was altered to 075° and proceeded at 9 knots to the new patrol area. (16)
12 Jan 1943 At 0601 A/12, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day in position 39°11'N, 14°15'E.

At 1216 A/12, course was altered to 090° to make landfall on the coast near Mount Cocuzzo (39°13'N, 16°08'E). It was intended to examine the coast from close inshore during the day of the 13th between 39°00'N and 39°20'N and close a likely place to gun a train during the night of 13/14 January after which it was intended to move to the northern end of the area and choose another place in the vicinity of 40°00'N for gunning a train during the night of 14/15 January.
From 1545 A/12 to 1735 A/12, distant intermittent depth charging was heard.
At 1828 A/12, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 39°15'N, 14°51'E.
At 2100 A/12, gun action was exercised. (16)

13 Jan 1943 At 0607 A/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day in position 39°12'N, 15°50'E and she then closed the shore to attempt to identify the land and obtain a fix. There was a strong westerly gale blowing with a heavy swell which made periscope watch off shore difficult and ineffective.
At 1052 A/13, a fix was obtained which placed P 44 off Paola in position 39°21'N, 15°50'E.

P 44 then proceeded on course 170° down the coast keeping about 3 miles from the shore while examining the railway line. Many iron bridges were seen close to the shore, all suitable places for gunning a train. No shipping was seen all day.

By 1520 A/13, the wind and sea were as bad if not worse than in the forenoon with no sign of improvement. It was decided that gunning a train that night would be out of the question. P 44 went to 70 feet, altered course to 270° and retired to seaward.

At 1826 A/13, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 39°14'N, 15°53'E. She remained hove to in the heavy sea and swell while charging the batteries. It was decided to remain about 10 to 15 miles from the coast until 0400 A/14 and if the weather still showed no signs of moderating she would then proceed westwards to patrol on the convoy route between Messina and Naples which passed 4 miles to the east of Stromboli.
At 1900 A/13, white flashes were seen in the direction of Paola. Further flasted were seen between 2135 A/14 and 2157 A/14.
At 2208 A/13, HE was heard approaching from astern. P 44 dived in position 39°12'N, 15°50'E. After diving HE of two small patrol vessels, probably motor torpedo boats, was picked up.
At 2222 A/13, the HE faded. P 44 proceeded further to seaward as it appeared she may have been detected.
At 2310 A/13, P 44 surfaced in position 39°12'N, 15°46'E and continued to retire to seaward.

14 Jan 1943 At 0400 A/14, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) proceeded further westwards to patrol on the Messina - Naples shipping route as the weather was still too rough for close inshore patrol and gunnery.
At 0557 A/14, P 44 dived for the day in position 39°12'N, 15°50'E and patrolled in the vicinity of that position all day but nothing was sighted.
Between 1200 A/14 and 1500 A/14 intermittent distant explosions were heard.
By 1600 A/14, the weather had started to moderate.
At 1828 A/14, P 44 surfaced in position 39°19'N, 15°06'E. The wind had dropped to force 1 and the swell had also decreased considerably which made gunnery possible.

Lt. Roxburgh decided to make the coast in the vicinity of 39°00'N and gin a train in view of the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1755 A/14 which gave P 44 and extra day in hand. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 14 January 1943.] It was also intended after the action to proceed 35 miles up the coast and gun another train in the early hours of the following night. (16)

14 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1755 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN). Leave patrol P.M. Jan. 15th and return Malta. HMS Una through position 180° - Ustica - 10 miles thence direct to point 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) and by usual route to arrive at Malta 1500 A/l8. HMS P 44 through position 360° - Ustica = 10 miles thence direct to position 'G' and by usual route to arrive at Malta 1300 A/l9. Speed to be adjusted to pass 'G', HMS Una at 2000 A/16 and HMS P 44 24 hours later. (42)

15 Jan 1943 At 0016 A/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) made landfall in position 39°02'N, 16°05'E. She was unable to identify the land owning to low cloud and darkness. The setting moon obscured the coast and the bridges and railway line were barely visible.

At 0130 A/15, having seen no trains of activity, decided to withdrew to seaward and return in daylight for reconnaissance prior to making a third attempt on gunning a train early on the night of the 15th. Almost as soon as P 44 had altered 180° away from the beach and was still relatively close a steam train passed. This missed opportunity was a bitter disappointment.

At 0300 A/15, petrol engine HE was picked up on bearing 100° which was most likely a motor torpedo boat. It would appear that there is a standing patrol of two motor torpedo boats near the coast in this vicinity.

At 0602 A/15, P 44 dived in position 39°10'N, 15°54'E. Set course 100° for inshore reconnaissance from periscope depth to find a suitable place to gun a train, bridge and / or viaduct early in the evening.

At 0857 A/15, a fix was obtained in position 39°06'N, 16°01'E. During the forenoon the wind increased once more and by noon a strong westerly gale was again blowing. The shore was not visible from time to time from 3 miles out by rainsqualls. P 44 remained patrolling about 3 miles from the shore in the hope the weather would moderate.
At 1647 A/15, the weather was still very bad so P 44 commenced to withdraw from the area as gunnery once more was out of the question.
At 1827 A/15, P 44 surfaced in position 39°02'N, 15°56'E. Course was set to 265° at 6 knots. Faster speed was not possible in the heavy seas.

16 Jan 1943 At 0556 A/16, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day in position 38°57'N, 11°30'E. She then proceeded on course 270° at 70 feet as continuous periscope depth patrol was not possible due to the heavy seas. She only came up of the W/T routines.
At 0939 A/16, the Capt S.10's signal timed 0836 A/16 was received in which HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was ordered to make the passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) two hours ahead of P 44.
At 1828 A/16, P 44 surfaced in position 38°59'N, 13°49'E. The weather had moderated considerable and continued to improve further during the night. (16)

17 Jan 1943 HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian destroyer Bombardiere (1654 tons, built 1942) about 20 nautical miles north-west of Marettimo Island, Sicily, Italy in position 38°04'N 11°49'E. She was escorting the Italian transport Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942) with her sister ship Legionario. The convoy was on passage from Bizerta to Palermo.

Legionario could not stop to pick up the survivors from fear she would become also a victim of the submarine and could only jettison eight life rafts. The Italian corvettes Antelope, Artemide and Gabbiano sailed from Trapani to search for survivors and found only three officers and forty ratings, all wounded. The cold water must have accounted for a large part of the 174 victims.

0601 A/17 - Dived in position 38°22'N, 12°09'E. The weather very good but the sea was glassy calm. It was decided to patrol at 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up only coming up for the W/T routines.
0942 A/17 - Sighted smoke to the southward.
1208 A/17 - Destroyer HE was picked up to the southward. The enemy was proceeding northwards. Nothing was sighted though.
1231 A/17 - Sighted smoke and heard HE to the south-east.
1455 A/17 - Destroyer HE was heard to the eastward. Came to periscope depth. Only six patrolling aircraft were sighted. There were no ships in sight.

1516 A/17 - Two loud explosions were heard quite close. Possibly one of the patrolling aircraft had detected the submarine and dropped bombs or depth charges. P 44 altered course to 217° which meant passing 15 miles east of position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) but from the HE and the smoke sighted during the forenoon it appeared that the shipping route to Bizerta was some 10 miles south-east of P 44 and parallel to her previous course.

1600 A/17 - Sighted the masts of three merchant vessels well below the horizon to the southward. Closed at speed. Unfortunately P 44 was abaft the beam of this convoy when they were sighted and so they passed out of range.
1644 A/17 - Broke off the attack and went to 90 feet.
[Campania convoy on passage from Palermo to Bizerta ???]
1705 A/17 - Heard fast diesel and turbine HE bearing 257°.
1715 A/17 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted a modern 7000-tons merchant vessel escorted by two fleet destroyers on a course of 048°. Enemy speed 13 knots. Started attack.
1727 A/17 - In position 38°04'N, 11°49'E fired four torpedoes at the merchant vessel from 3000 yards. Went deep on firing and went off the tracks.
1729 A/17 - Heard one torpedo explosion. All HE of the merchant ship ceased and was not heard again.
1733 A/17 - A counter attack started. 30 Depth charges were dropped in the next 30 minutes. P 44 meanwhile withdrew to the west keeping stern on to the transmitting destroyer.
1807 A/17 - HE of the destroyers disappeared. More HE was later heard to the southward forcing P 44 to return to the area of the attack.
1948 A/17 - HE faded out.
2224 A/17 - Surfaced in position 38°07'N, 11°41'E. Sighted a stopped motor torpedo boat about 3/4 mile away to the north-west. Dived immediately. The hatch was open a bare minute.
2240 A/17 - The E-boat got underway and started transmitting on Asdic.

2309 A/17 - Two destroyers and a second motor torpedo boat now arrived on the scene from the south-west. P 44 was hunted all through the night preventing her from surfacing to charge the battery and ventilate the boat with fresh air.
0300 A/18 - HE faded out to the north-west.
0400 A/18 - Just when debating on surfacing for a quick charge and letting some fresh air into the boat, HE was heard to the northward.
0435 A/18 - Classified the HE as motor torpedo boats hunting P 44.
0515 A/18 - A destroyer now joined the motor torpedo boats and the hunt continued for 1.5 hours which prevented P 44 from surfacing and she was thus forced to spend another day submerged.
0650 A/18 - All HE had now disappeared to the northward. P 44 had now been hunted and depth charged for 13 hours.

0715 A/18 - Heard HE approaching on a steady bearing and sighted the smoke of a convoy bearing 085°. As the torpedoes had not been reloaded during the hunt, and as Lt. Roxburgh had decided to save the battery as much as possible for the prolonged dive, P 44 went deep to let the convoy pass. [possibly the Ankara-convoy ???]
0800 A/18 - The southbound convoy passed overhead. It consisted of at least ten ships including the escort.
P 44 remained deep throughout the day only coming up for W/T routines. Course was set to 200°. Numerous explosions were heard but none were close.
0933 A/18 - While at periscope depth a 200-tons southbound schooner was sighted at a range of about a mile. [San Francisco di Paola ??? on passage from Trapani to Bizerta.]
1826 A/18 - Surfaced in position 37°45'N, 11°32'E after having been submerged for 36.5 hours. Many of the crew were suffering from carbon monoxide poisoning.
1923 A/18 - Dived for an aircraft, surfacing again at 1952 A/18.
2005 A/18 - Sighted an enemy convoy being attack to the westward by Allied aircraft. [XXI Aprile-convoy on passage from Tunis to Palermo ???]
2232 A/18 - In position 37°30'N, 11°12'E, sighted two motor torpedo boats. Dived and altered course to 140°. P 44 surfaced at 0053 A/19 in position 37°21'N, 11°16'E. (16)

19 Jan 1943 At 0548 A/19, in position 37°07'N, 11°35'E, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day and commenced reloading the torpedo tubes now that the crew had recovered some of their strength.
At 0654 A/19, the reload was completed.
At 1404 A/19, HE was picked up to the south-east which later drew astern.
At 1442 A/19, S.S./T. calls were exchanged with HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN), which had been the source of the HE.
At 1859 A/19, P 44 surfaced in position 36°27'N, 11°48'E but she immediately had to dive again for an aircraft.
At 1915 A/19, HMS Rorqual was seen on bearing 300° through the periscope proceeding on the surface. Range was about 2 miles.
At 2102 A/19, HMS P 44 surfaced in position 36°26'N, 11°55'E and proceeded at 10 knots for Malta.
At 2305 A/19, a signal timed, 2146 A/19 was sent to the Capt. S.10. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (16)
20 Jan 1943 Around 1430 A/20, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (also 11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

1 Feb 1943 Around 1300 A/1, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (also 12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Tunisia between 35°00'N and 36°00'N.
Three hours after departure a gunnery exercise was carried out.
She was to proceed through position 36°00'N, 14°05'E en-route though her patrol area.
3 Feb 1943 At 0600 A/3, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived for the day in position 35°28'N, 11°14'E.
At 0700 A/3, landfall was made near Mahdia and patrol was commenced along the coast to the southward.
At 1630 A/3, P 44 began to retire to seaward.
At 1900 A/3, P 44 surfaced in position 35°15'N, 11°17'E and continued proceeding seawards to patrol 15 miles from the coast.
At 1940 A/3, HE from a fast petrol engine was heard inshore of P 44. It is thought this was a southbound motor torpedo boat. (16)

6 Feb 1943 At 2114 A/6, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1131 A/6 which contained the following orders; After dark tonight, unless in pursuit of the enemy withdraw to vicinity of 36°00'N, 12°00'E and at your discretion make brief report on volume of shipping sighted. Return subsequently to your area.
At 2130 A/6, P 44 set course to 090° and increased to full speed to the position ordered.
At 0218 A/7, the signal timed 0101 A/7 was passed to the Capt. S.10. which contained the following; Your 1131 A/6. Absolutely nothing sighted between 35°00'N and 35°48'N.
In a reply timed 1140 A/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to P 44; Your 0101 A/7 acknowledged. Do not neglect Sousse. (43)

8 Feb 1943 From 1143 A/8 to 1151 A/8, When patrolling submerged in position 049° - Sousse breakwater light - 1.65 miles, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) the periscope was taken under fire by a shore battery. The periscope obviously must have been sighted.
The gunfire was very accurate and caused some minor defects to her Asdic training gear and log.
P 44 went to 60 feet and withdrew at speed on course 050°.
At 1424 A/8, the HE of what was possibly a motor torpedo boat was heard proceeding in the direction of Sousse.
Between 1458 A/8 and 1645 A/8, distant explosions were heard.

At 1903 A/8, P 44 surfaced to the north of Kuriat in position 35°56'N, 11°01'E. Lt. Roxburgh decided to patrol from Kuriat to the southwards during the coming days as the Sousse area seemed to be compromised also due to the successes of HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) in the second half of the previous month. (16)

10 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1322 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y.
