Roxburgh, DSC, RN); During daylight tomorrow 11th, P 44 patrol in northwest corner of your area. P 43 patrol in [position garbled in the signal] after 0100 A/11th. (44)

11 Feb 1943 At 2057 A/11, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 A/11 which contained the following orders for P 44; Extend patrol area to 36°40'N, after 2300 A/12 and leave patrol after dark/13. Pass 20 miles north of Linosa through position 36°05'N, 14°05'E to position '6'. Make the usual rendezvous off Malta at 0830 A/15. (43)

12 Feb 1943 In position 38°58'N, 10°40'E, in poor visibility, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a 1500-tons merchant vessel escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat. An attack was attempted but was thwarted by the enemy making an alteration of course which brought it in near collision with the submarine.

This was the German transport Skotfoss (former Norwegian, 1465 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Pallade on passage from Palermo to Sousse. They had been in company with the small German transport Jaedjoer (308 GRT, built 1937) but the latter had lost contact with them and had been sunk by HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) the previous evening.
0558 A/12 - Dived in position 35°58'N, 10°47'E. Patrolled at 60 feet as there was still a northerly gale blowing. Only came up for W/T routines.
0917 A/12 - Came to periscope depth for a W/T routine. Visibility through the periscope were very poor.

0924 A/12 - In position 35°58'N, 10°40'E, sighted a 1500-tons merchant vessel escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat about a mile away. Enemy course was 150°, speed 8 knots. The target was soon lost in the bad weather conditions.

0930 A/12 - The enemy was seen again apparently having made a navigational alteration. P 44 was dead ahead of the target and had to go deep to avoid being rammed and the merchant vessel and torpedo boat subsequently passed overhead.
0934 A/12 - Returned to periscope depth. HE was heard quite close but there was no sign of the enemy.
0941 A/12 - Went deep again as the enemy could not be seen. It is considered that the HE of the enemy had not been picked up earlier due to water noises from the shore.
1902 A/12 - Surfaced in position 35°59'N, 10°46'E.

13 Feb 1943 At 1901 A/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) surfaced in position 36°24'N, 10°49'E, after a day of submerged patrol near Nabeul during which nothing was sighted. She then left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 A/11. (16)

15 Feb 1943 Around 0900 A/15, HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) made rendezvous with off the Malta swept channel with HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which then swept her in. She subsequently ended her 12th war patrol (also 12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta later that morning. (45)
27 Feb 1943 At 1500 A/27, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (also 13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation.
A party of four officers and one army private from COPP III was embarked. They were to conduct beach reconnaissance for Operation Husky, the upcoming invasion of Sicily.
For the daily positions of HMS United during this patrol see the map below.

28 Feb 1943 In the evening, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) landed a folbot with two of COPP III's party (Lt. P.R.G. Smith of the Royal Engineers and Lt. D. Brand, RNVR) off Gela, Sicily for an inspection of the beach. They successfully did so and returned later the same evening.
0805 A/28 - Made landfall off Gela. Proceeded four miles off shore for a periscope reconnaissance of beaches '23' and '24'. Due to the shallow water it was not possible to close further.
1251 A/28 - Lt. Smith was satisfied with the reconnaissance. United bottomed in position 36°57.8'N, 14°16'E in 96 feet of water.
1807 A/28 - Came to periscope depth and proceeded inshore on course 014° to bottom in the Folbot launching position, 37°00'N, 14°16.5'E.
1911 A/28 - Bottomed in position 37°00'N, 14°16.5'E in 49 feet of water. From this position the Folbot had to pull 2.75 miles to the beach.
1920 A/28 - Surfaced and launched the Folbot, ten minutes later, with Lt's Smith and Brand. They were to return at midnight. The weather was perfect.
2001 A/28 - Bottomed in the launching position, pointed to seaward.
2320 A/28 - Surfaced.
2330 A/28 - Commenced the homing procedure.
2335 A/28 - Sighted the Folbot.
2350 A/28 - The Folbot was recovered and withdrew to seaward. (16)

1 Mar 1943 In the evening, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) landed a folbot with two of COPP III's party off Gela (again Smith and Brand), Sicily for an inspection of the beach. They failed to return early the next morning.
In fact, they had made it back to Malta when they could not find the submarine.
0530 A/1 - Dived in position 36°53'N, 14°07'E and commenced to close the coast.
1132 A/1 - Bottomed in position 36°58'N, 14°15'E in 100 feet of water.
1807 A/1 - Came to periscope depth and proceeded inshore on course 038° to bottom in the Folbot launching position.
1913 A/1 - Bottomed in position 37°00'N, 14°16.5'E in 47 feet of water.
1920 A/1 - Surfaced and launched the Folbot 10 minutes later. It was expected they would return to the submarine around 0100 A/2.
1945 A/1 - Dived and bottomed.
2115 A/1 - Heard 3 or 4 distant explosions in quick succession.
0020 A/2 - Surfaced.
0030 A/2 - Commenced the homing procedure.
0130 A/2 - No sign of the Folbot as of yet. As all was quiet ashore the homing procedure was continued.

0212 A/2 - Two sharp explosions were heard on the Asdic. This was a pre-arranged signal with the Folbot party to surface the submarine in an emergency and take them on board. The Folbot was however not sighted and the explosions were probably a coincidence.
0245 A/2 - The Folbot party was now one hour and forty-five minutes overdue and course was set to retire to seaward. Course was set to the second emergency rendezvous in position 36°56'N, 14°10'E.
Between 0308 A/2 and 0316 A/2 - White flares were observed ashore 7 miles to the south-east of the Folbot landing position.
0446 A/2 - Further white flares were observed ashore 12 miles south-east of the Folbot landing position.
0527 A/2 - Dived in position 36°58'N, 14°12'E and searched the area within 5 miles of the emergency rendezvous position.
During the day three aircraft were seen patrolling the area from time to time.
1903 A/2 - Surfaced in position 36°57'N, 14°09'E and kept on patrolling in the vicinity of the emergency rendezvous position.
2038 A/2 - Dived in position 36°54'N, 14°13'E for an aircraft which flew overhead. United surfaced at 0001 A/3 in position 36°56'N, 14°10'E. (16)

3 Mar 1943 At 0531 A/3, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°58'N, 14°06'E and began withdrawing to the southern limit of the operations area to report the failure of Lt's Smith and Brand to return to the submarine and that beach '23' may be compromised and also it was the intention to proceed to beach '18A' with the remainder of the party.
At 1933 A/3, HMS United surfaced in position 36°35'N, 13°55'E and continued proceeding southwards.

At 2007 A/3, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1725 A/3 was received which reported the arrival of the party at Malta. Lt. Roxburgh did not want to return to the Gela area but Lt. Hart was keen on returning to the area to reconnoitre beach '24' which had the next priority. It was decided to proceed there provided that all was quiet.
At 2144 A/3, course was altered to 030°.

At 2155 A/3, HMS United dived in position 36°12'N, 13°45'E to shake off a persistent aircraft contact which remained patrolling rather close to the submarine. It was thought this was a British aircraft.

At 2300 A/3, HMS United surfaced in position 36°23'N, 13°36'E and proceeded on course 030°. There was now contact on three to four aircraft but Lt. Roxburgh decided to ignore these as they were very likely all British. (16)

4 Mar 1943 At 0528 A/4, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°47'N, 14°15'E. Visibility was very poor due to the haze and the land was not visible until the submarine was only five miles from it.
At 0945 A/4, in position 36°50'N, 14°20'E, two minesweepers were sighted proceeding on course 290°.

At 1102 A/4, when in position 36°55'N, 14°20'E, two very loud explosions occurred. The first one very close to the submarine. Nothing was apparently in sight at the time. Course was altered and the submarine went deep.

At 1121 A/4, a third explosion occurred but a bit further away. Lt. Roxburgh was convinced the submarine had been sighted at periscope depth by an aircraft which had dropped bombs. He considered the area compromised. It was therefore decided to withdraw and reconnoitre beach '18A' during the following night.
At 1933 A/4, HMS United surfaced in position 36°47'N, 14°07'E. She then patrolled in the vicinity of this position while charging. (16)

5 Mar 1943 At 0512 A/5, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°43'N, 14°21'E and proceeded on course 100°, 3 miles from the shore towards beach '18A'. On the way beaches '21', '20' and '19' were inspected. The wind was blowing force 5 to 6 which made Folbot-work out of the question.

At 1255 A/5, HMS United bottomed in position 36°43'N, 14°40'E, 1.5 miles from beach '18A' in 13 fathoms of water. Before bottoming both members of the party that were to conduct the next Folbot operation inspected beach '18A' through the periscope.
At 1650 A/5, the submarine came to periscope depth. The wind was still blowing strongly from the east. All hope of landing the Folbot this night was now abandoned.
At 1700 A/5, HMS United began withdrawing from the coast to get out of range from the radar station in the vicinity.
At 2311 A/5, HMS United surfaced in position 36°38'N, 14°25'E. (16)

6 Mar 1943 At 0529 A/6, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°38'N, 14°26'E and proceeded along the coast to beach '18A'. The bad weather was still persistent although the wind had now veered to the south-east.
At 1025 A/6, HMS United bottomed in position 36°42'N, 14°40'E in 19 fathoms of water.
At 1650 A/6, HMS United came to periscope depth. The wind had veered to the south but the weather conditions were still unsuitable for 'Folboting'.
At 1700 A/6, course was altered to 250° to withdraw from the coast.
At 1712 A/6, an aircraft was seen patrolling inshore along the coast.
At 2245 A/6, HMS United surfaced in position 36°38'N, 14°27'E and charged the battery in this vicinity. (16)
7 Mar 1943 At 0522 A/7, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°38'N, 14°29'E and commenced to close the coast.
At 1040 A/7, HMS United bottomed in position 36°42'N, 14°39'E in 17.5 fathoms.

At 1648 A/7, HMS United came to periscope depth. The wind had dropped but there was still a very heavy swell running from the south-west. 'Folboting' might have been possible but it was decided to wait for an additional day for more favourable weather conditions so commenced to withdraw from the coast.
At 2300 A/7, HMS United surfaced in position 36°32'N, 14°31'E and commenced charging the battery. (16)

8 Mar 1943 In the evening, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) landed a folbot with two of COPP III's party (Lieutenant Hart and Sub Lieutenant Folder) off Cava d'Aliga, Sicily for an inspection of the beach.
0526 A/8 - Dived in position 36°36'N, 14°30'E and closed the coast.
1033 A/8 - Bottomed in position 36°42'N, 14°39'E in 19 fathoms of water.

1657 A/8 - Came to periscope depth. The wind had freshened slightly and was blowing force 4 from the east but the swell had died down since the previous night. Though not ideal 'Folboting' weather it was decided to go ahead with the landing on beach '18A'.
1725 A/8 - Began closing the beach to bottom in the Folbot launching position 36°43'N, 14°40'E which was 1.25 miles from the beach.

2033 A/8 - Surfaced and launched the Folbot 10 minutes later manned by Lt. Hart and Sub.Lt. Folder. There was quite a sea running and wind was force 4. Visibility was good but it was very dark. The shore showed up well. The Folbot party expected to return at 2330 A/8.
2105 A/8 - Bottomed in the launching position.
2307 A/8 - Surfaced. The wind had increased slightly to force 5.
2400 A/8 - The Folbot was now half an hour overdue but the homing procedure was continued. [Continued in the event for 9 March 1943.] (16)

9 Mar 1943 While waiting for the return of the Folbot party, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) missed an opportunity to sink the Italian submarine Filippo Corridoni transferring from Augusta to Trapani. The enemy submarine passed only 500 yards ahead but United had orders not to attack the enemy while recovering the COPP party. Unfortunately, the Folbot failed to return.
0036 A/9 - An underwater explosion was reported by the Asdic operator.

0140 A/9 - Two underwater explosions were heard by the Asdic operator. They sounded like the S.U.E. charges carried by the Folbot party. In view of these charges, Lt. Roxburgh decided to remain in the vicinity and continue the homing procedure and not to proceed away from the coast as had previously been arranged. All was quiet ashore. Lt. Roxburgh thought the Folbot to be near but unable to find the submarine.

0204 A/9 - Sighted a submarine to seaward proceeding west along the coast at a range of about half a mile. United was stopped and pointed out to sea at the time the submarine crossed her bow on a track of about 80° - 90° at a range of only 500 yards. In accordance with the operations order this tempting target was not attacked.

0236 A/9 - United bottomed at 116 feet. Lt. Roxburgh had decided not to proceed to the backup rendezvous position as it was very dark and chances of sighting the Folbot were very slim. He decided to proceed along their route during daylight keeping a good lookout for the Folbot party through the periscope.

0555 A/9 - Came to periscope depth. There was no sign of the Folbot. Unfortunately the weather had worsened considerably. Proceeded on course 210° for 10 miles and then altered course to 190° which was the Folbot's subsequent course for Malta.
1130 A/9 - Still no sign of the Folbot. Wind was now force 7 with a heavy sea and swell running from the west. The Folbot's only chance in this weather was to heave to.
1135 A/9 - Began to clear the area at speed to inform the Capt. S.10 of the situation.
1333 A/9 - Surfaced in position 36°28'N, 14°19'E to pass a signal to the Capt. S.10.
1408 A/9 - Passed United's signal timed 1331 A/9 to the Capt. S.10 reporting the failure of the Folbot party to return and requesting an air search. Also made an E.T.A. at Malta.
1410 A/9 - Dived in position 36°26'N, 14°14'E.
2026 A/9 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1543 A/9 in which he acknowledged receiving the signal timed 1331 A/9.
10 Mar 1943 At 1350 A/10, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (also 13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

21 Mar 1943 Around 1500 A/21, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (also 14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily. The exact patrol area would be signalled while on passage.
A few hours after departure gunnery exercises were, briefly, carried out.

22 Mar 1943 At 2044 A/22, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1316 A/22 which contained the following orders for her; Pass position 'P' at 1200 A/23 and proceed to patrol in Zone 'D', west of 12°44'E. The western half of this zone may prove profitable.

At 2249 A/22, an aircraft report was received of two medium sized transports and two destroyers in the Gulf of Hammamet at 2230 A/22 in position 36°00'N, 10°00'E, course 040°, speed 6 knots. [The position in the signal was however on land.]

At 0008 A/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2316 A/22 was received which contained the following information / orders; At 2230 A/22 convoy in position 36°05'N, 11°06'E, 040°, 6 knots. Air co-operation commenced 2230 hours, torpedo aircraft strike expected about 0130 A/23. Proceed to intercept south of 37°05'N. [The position of the convoy had now been corrected and they were off the Gulf of Hammamet.]
At 0016 A/23, course was altered to 180° and it was estimated United was about 60 miles to the north of this convoy.
[Skotfoss-convoy from Sousse to Trapani ???] (46)

23 Mar 1943 At 0135 A/23, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2357 A/22 in which she was ordered to proceed in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1316 A/22 if the convoy had not been sighted by 1200 A/23.
Meanwhile in a signal timed 2333 A/22, the Capt. S.10 had also informed United that Allied motor torpedo boat were also trying to intercept the convoy.
At 0516 A/23, an aircraft flare was seen bearing 250°. This was the first flare sighted and this was encouraging as no further amplifying reports had been received since the signal timed 2316 A/22.
At 0520 A/23, course was altered to 260°.
At 0533 A/23, United dived in position 35°46'N, 11°21'E as it was getting quite light. These was still no sign of the reported convoy. Course 260° was still being steered but now submerged.
At 0631 A/23, two distant explosions were heard but there was still nothing in sight at this time.

At 0713 A/23, HMS United obtained a fix which placed her in position 36°49'N, 11°17'E, 7.5 miles to the east of Kelibia. She then patrolled in this vicinity until 1200 A/23 but nothing was sighted. During this time numerous bits of wreckage were sighted and also a petrol can which looked similar to an upper deck fuel can carried by British motor torpedo boats.
At 1200 A/23, HMS United commenced to withdraw from the area as per the orders given in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2357 A/22.
At 1930 A/23, HMS United surfaced in position 37°03'N, 11°28'E and set course for position 'P' steering a course of 002°.
At 2027 A/23, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1306 A/23 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol in zone 'D', keeping west of 12°44'E.

At 2155 A/23, a dark object was sighted to the eastward. HMS United turned away and dived in position 37°12'N, 11°28'E. From the HE of the object it was thought to be a U-class submarine. As HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC, RN) was expected in the area, HMS United surfaced at 2204 A/23 and two minutes later the other submarine was challenged. Two minutes after the challenge the other submarine dived. HMS Unseen was under the impression that she had encountered a motor torpedo boat. (46)
24 Mar 1943 At 0400 A/24, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) passed position 'P'.
At 0500 A/24, United dived to 70 feet in position 38°08'N, 11°41'E.
At 0502 A/24, the HE of a destroyer was picked up drawing to the port side at 22 knots. Before diving there was nothing in sight although visibility was between 5 to 6 miles.
Between 0502 A/24 and 0545 A/24, the HE of approximately six destroyers all doing 22 knots passed down the port side and faded astern.
At 1933 A/24, HMS United surfaced in position 38°36'N, 11°49'E.
At 2034 A/24, HE of a motor torpedo boat was picked up to the southward. It was proceeding to the east at speed. HE was lost after 5 minutes.
At 2300 A/24, HMS United commenced to patrol up and down the moon in area 'D'. (16)
25 Mar 1943 At 0511 A/25, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived in position 38°58'N, 11°49'E. She then patrolled on courses 270° - 090° through position 39°00'N, 12°00'E.
At 1930 A/25, HMS United surfaced in position 38°59'N, 11°54'E.
At 2025 A/25, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1152 A/25 was received which ordered her to patrol in zone 'A' from 0500 A/26.
At 2100 A/25, course was set to 180° to proceed to zone 'A'. (47)
27 Mar 1943 At 0457 A/27, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived to the north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°21'N, 12°22'E and continued closing the coast.
At 0902 A/27, HMS United commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 270° - Cape San Vito lighthouse - 5 miles (38°11'N, 12°37'E).
At 1730 A/27, HMS United began to withdraw from the coast. No shipping at all had been sighted during the day.
At 2000 A/27, HMS United surfaced in position 38°20'N, 12°26'E. She then patrolled in the vicinity of that position with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 2027 A/27, HE was picked up on bearing 150°. This was reported as being diesel. The bearing changed rapidly and within 5 minutes it faded on bearing 230°. Nothing was seen from the bridge. The night was clear but very dark. It was first thought to be from a submarine but was later thought to have been a diesel engined motor torpedo boat.
The following day, HMS United again patrolled of Cape San Vito but just like on the 27th, no enemy shipping was sighted. (16)

29 Mar 1943 At 0506 A/29, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived north-west of Cape San Vito in position 38°22'N, 12°23'E and then closed the coast again to patrol 5 miles to the west of Cape San Vito.
At 1959 A/29, HMS United surfaced in position 38°18'N, 12°30'E, again having sighted nothing all day.

At 2037 A/29, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1156 A/29 was received which contained the following orders for her; Leave patrol for Malta passing position 'P' at 0530 A/31, thence as ordered by the Capt. S.10.
At 2200 A/29, course was set to 270° for a patrol position 10 to 15 miles to the north-west of Marettimo since patrol off Cape San Vito had proven fruitless for the past few days. (47)
30 Mar 1943 At 0110 A/30, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted an aircraft flare bearing 065°.
At 0123 A/30, an aircraft report, timed 0100 A/30 was received which reported three motor torpedo boats in position 38°24'N, 12°33'E. This was only 10 miles bearing 070° from United's position.

At 0126 A/30, another aircraft flare was sighted bearing 070° and at the same time red and white pyrotechnic lights were seen. All this was on the same bearing in which the enemy motor torpedo boats were reported. Therefore these flares were not closed for further investigation. It was presumed that, if any merchant vessels were present, the aircraft would report so.
At 0340 A/30, HMS United dived, to the north of Marettimo, in position 38°20'N, 12°05'E.
At 0350 A/30, nine distant single explosions were heard, as of motor torpedo boats dropping single depth charges.
At 0650 A/30, thirteen distant single explosions were heard.
During the day, HMS United patrolled to the north-west of Marettimo, in the vicinity of position 38°08'N, 11°53'E. Nothing, except a few aircraft, was seen all day.

At 1203 A/30, the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1144 A/30 was received which contained the following information; Aircraft report two enemy medium size merchant ships and four destroyers in position 38°27'N, 11°26'E, course 200°, speed 5 knots at 0956 A/30.
[Belluno-convoy aka 'convoy RR' on passage from Naples to Tunis ???]

At 1400 A/30, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1202 A/30 was received which stated that HMS United may have been sighted at to the north-west of Cape San Vito at 0730 A/30. At this time United was 14 miles to the north-north-west of Marettimo and 35 miles due west of Cape San Vito so it was considered that it was not HMS United that had been sighted. Possibly the thirteen explosions heard at 0650 A/30 had something to do with this sighting report.

At 1638 A/30, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1541 A/30 was received which contained the following information; Six merchant ships, three destroyers, two motor torpedo boats, at 1420 A/30 in position 39°40'N, 12°22'E, course 220°, speed 10 knots.
This course took the convoy down the western edge of the Skerki Bank minefield to Bizerta.
At 1702 A/30, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1623 A/30 was received which contained the following information; My 1541 A/30. Enemy last observed in position 39°33'N, 12°00'E, course 142° at 1610 A/30.

If either of these reports were correct, the convoy would not prove a target for HMS United. However it was very likely that the enemy was Bizerta bound and would pass somewhere between Marettimo and the eastern edge of the Skerki Bank minefield. In any way they were not due to cross 38°00'N until after 0200 A/31. Lt. Roxburgh therefore decided to patrol west of Marettimo in position 38°00'N, 11°40'E which was midway between Marettimo and the Skerki Bank minefield and it was hoped that aircraft reports during the night would indicate which route the convoy was taking. At mist, if the convoy was going down either edge of this gap, HMS United had only to proceed 20 miles either east or west in order to intercept.
At 1954 A/30, HMS United surfaced in position 38°04'N, 11°54'E and set course 245° to proceed to the chosen patrol position. (47)

31 Mar 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a convoy of three merchant ships and two destroyers but was thwarted in her attack when a destroyer turned towards her and she was forced to dive.

The convoy encountered was most likely the one made up of the Italian transports Belluno (former French Fort de France, 4279 GRT, built 1935) and Pierre Claude (former French, 1794 GRT, built 1934), escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Fortunale, Antares, Enrico Cosenz and Sagittario on passage from Naples (with a stopover in the Favignana anchorage) to Tunis.
0200 A/31 - Sighted an aircraft flare bearing 060° at a range of about 30 miles.

0209 A/31 - Received an aircraft report timed 0205 A/31 which reported one merchant vessel and two destroyers in position 38°13'N, 12°21'E, course 230°, speed 10 knots. This was either a different convoy to that one reported at 1658 A/30 or part of it after it had split up. The position was 31 miles to the east-north-east of HMS United. The course would take them 1.5 miles from Marettimo. It was decided to try to intercept the convoy around 0600 A/31 in position 37°48'N, 11°41'E and carry out a daylight submerged attack. This position was only 14 miles United's current position.
0225 A/31 - Altered course to 192° and increased speed to 5 knots.
0242 A/31 - Sighted an aircraft flare bearing 062° at a range of about 25 miles.
0310 A/31 - Sighted an aircraft flare bearing 065° at a range of about 20 miles.

0345 A/31 - Sighted a convoy of three 5000-tons merchant vessels and two destroyers bearing 030° at a range of 4000 yards. This was totally unexpected and was probably the other portion of the original convoy. Enemy course was 235° and it was about 20 miles ahead of the convoy United was now making for. United was 90° on the port bow of the convoy and was in line ahead with one destroyer weaving ahead and one weaving astern. The submarine was unfavourably placed with regard to the moon. United was manoeuvring to keep the stern on when the rear destroyer suddenly turned towards and commenced to close at speed.

0350 A/31 - Dived in position 37°54'N, 11°42'E with the destroyer still closing at a range of only 1500 yards. The Asdic operator reported the speed of the convoy as 8 to 10 knots. Both destroyers were transmitting on Asdic. The HE of the rear destroyer passed very close astern but she did not drop any depth charges.

0430 A/31 - The destroyer HE had drawn clear and the convoy was now out of range so it was decided to continue, attempting to intercept the reported merchant vessel and two destroyers at 0600 A/31. United proceeded to the intended patrol position at 5 knots submerged.
0541 A/31 - Came to periscope depth in position 37°49'N, 11°39'E. There was nothing in sight. She then patrolled on course 180° until 0700 A/31 but nothing was sighted.
0700 A/31 - Set course for position 'Q'.
Numerous distant explosions were heard throughout the day.
1940 A/31 - Surfaced in position 37°23'N, 11°35'E and set course to proceed past Pantelleria.
2035 A/31 - Received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1530 A/31 that a submarine had been sighted to the west of Marettimo at 1105 A/31. At this time United was 28 miles to the south-west of Marettimo.
2 Apr 1943 At 1030 A/2, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (also 14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0830 A/2. (16)

15 Apr 1943 Around 1200 B/15, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (also 15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south coast of Italy.
Before proceeding on patrol A/S exercises were carried out with HMS Pakenham (Cdr. B. Jones, DSC, RN) and HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN). The exercises were completed at 1730 B/15.
[No log is available for this period so map for this patrol can be displayed.] (16)

18 Apr 1943 At 1241 B/18, off Cape Spartivento, in position 37°53'N, 16°05'E, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boat proceeding westwards close inshore. A small auxiliary armed schooner of about 100-tons also passed inshore, but this vessel was eastbound.
At 1925 B/18, a ship was sighted below the horizon to the south-west. This ship was on an easterly course. HMS United closed at speed for about an hour.

At 2029 B/18, the ship was identified as a fully lit hospital ship of about 5000-tons proceeding on a course of 098° at 10 knots. Range was 7500 yards. Position was 37°41'N, 16°00'E. The attack was broken off and HMS United altered course to 180°.
[Possibly Aquileia on passage from La Galouette to Bari ???]
At 2055 B/18, HMS United surfaced and patrolled up and down moon keeping about 10 miles from the coast. (16)

19 Apr 1943 At 2117 B/19, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1835 B/19 in which she was ordered to cover the southern approaches to the Straits of Messina as it was considered possible that enemy fleet units would proceed from La Spezia to the Adriatic.

At 2300 B/19, course was set to proceed to a position 7 miles to the south-west of Cape dell'Armi. [37°57'N, 15°41'E is given in the patrol report but this position does not correspond with the text given in the patrol report.] (48)
20 Apr 1943 At 1034 B/20, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0913 B/20 in which his orders as given in his signal timed 1835 B/19 were cancelled.

Around 1145 B/20, the masts of auxiliary schooner were seen inshore. The vessel was proceeding westwards. Soon afterwards this schooner was seen to anchor close inshore to the south-east of Cape dell'Armi in position 37°55.5'N, 15°43.2'E. HMS United closed with the intention to fire a torpedo at it but it was later seen the vessel was smaller than first thought and the plan was abandoned.

Shortly before 1700 B/20, an Italian Spica-class torpedo boat was seen astern. The enemy was proceeding eastwards and appeared to be on an A/S patrol. No HE was picked up due to the poor Asdic conditions. HMS United kept stern on but was forced to go deep several times due to a patrolling enemy flying boat. (48)

21 Apr 1943 At 2107 A/21, shortly after surfacing about 12 miles to the south-south-east of Cape dell'Armi, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a small object astern. Slow HE was picked up moving across the stern of the submarine between bearings 305° and 324°. HMS Upright was up moon and would have been sharply silhouetted had she altered course. The object was too small to be a merchant vessel and the Asdic operator was confident that the HE that was picked up was not from a merchant vessel. Lt. Roxburgh kept stern on and HMS United proceeded quietly away. (16)
22 Apr 1943 At 0034 B/22, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) picked up HE bearing 064°.

At 0049 B/22, the source of the HE was identified as a destroyer or torpedo boat doing about 22 knots. Bearing of the contact was now 075°. The moon was well up and the visibility was good but nothing was sighted.

At 0815 B/22 in position 38°00’N, 16°13’E, HMS United sighted what was at first believed to be a convoy but was then seen to be two Spica-class and one Generale-class torpedo boats moving slowly to the northeast. The submarine believed they were carrying an A/S sweep and it was decided not to attack.
This were the Italian torpedo boats Tifone and Giuseppe Dezza escorting the damaged torpedo boat Aretusa which was being towed by the tug Tifeo from Messina to Taranto.

23 Apr 1943 At 1045 B/23, off Punta Stilo, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a 150-tons armed auxiliary schooner patrolling in the area. The schooner was proceeding very slowly on course 220°, apparently using listening devices. She passed about one mile inshore of the submarine and eventually disappeared to the south-west.
At 1512 B/23, the same schooner was seen again but now returning on an opposite course.
At 2114 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1039 B/23 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol by reverse of outward route to arrive at Malta at 0830 B/27.
The same schooner was seen patrolling off Punta Stilo during the forenoon of the 24th. (16)
24 Apr 1943 At 2052 B/24, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) surfaced after a day of submerged patrol off Punta Stilo. She then left patrol to return to Malta. (16)
27 Apr 1943 At 1030 B/27, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (also 15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
9 May 1943 At 1400 B/9, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (also 16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation.

A party of 10 SAS troops (under command of Major J.G. Appleyard, DSO, MC) was to be landed on Pantelleria. After they had completed their objective they were to be taken off again (Operation Snapdragon).
[No log is available for this period so map for this patrol can be displayed.] (49)

10 May 1943 At 0401 B/10, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived 10 miles to the east of Pantelleria and closed to inspect the landing point at Punta Karace and Punta Polacca from between one and three miles from the shore. The weather conditions were unsuitable for a landing operation though.
At 2000 B/10, it was decided that the weather was still unsuitable and the operation for this night was cancelled. Course was then set to retire to the eastward of Pantelleria.
At 2226 B/10, HMS United surfaced 10 miles to the east of Pantelleria and commenced charging the battery.

At 2353 B/10, an aircraft report was received which reported two enemy vessels in position 36°58'N, 13°04'E. Enemy course was 210°, speed 10 knots. In accordance with the sailing orders no action was taken against these vessels which were probably bound for Lampedusa. (16)

11 May 1943 During the night, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted many aircraft flares being dropped. It was suspected these were dropped near the ships that had been reported the previous evening.
At 0439 B/11, HMS United dived to close the landing zone for a periscope reconnaissance.
At 1200 B/11, HMS United began to withdraw to patrol 8 miles from the coast.
Between 1745 B/11 and 2025 B/11, very numerous distant explosions were heard.

At 2134 B/11, HMS United surfaced 9 miles to the south-east of the landing zone and charged the battery. The weather conditions were still far from ideal but it was hoped that a sufficient lee could be obtained close inshore so that the operation could be continued.
At 2339 B/11, HMS United dived to close the coast submerged as the RDF station on Pantelleria had been sweeping.
At 0145 B/12, HMS United surfaced in position 090° - Karace Point - 1 mile. It was found the weather conditions were unsuitable and Lt. Roxburgh and Major Appleyard decided to cancel the operation.
At 0149 B/12, HMS United dived and retired from the coast. (16)
12 May 1943 At 0415 B/12, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) surfaced to ventilate the boat.
At 0445 B/12, HMS United dived and set course to return to Malta.
At 1905 B/12, HMS United surfaced and passed her signal timed 1901 B/12 in which she reported that the operation had not been carried out. She also reported her expected E.T.A. at Malta.
At 2108 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1643 B/12 was received in which the return route to Malta was amended due to a U-boat hunt ongoing in the normal submarine sanctuary.
At 2231 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2021 B/12 was received in which HMS United was ordered to confirm with his signal timed 1643 B/12. (16)
13 May 1943 At 1015 B/13, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (also 16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

She, and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), entered through the northern swept channel and were escorted in by three minesweepers [not identified in the patrol reports of both submarines] which had joined at 0830 B/13. (49)
14 May 1943 At 1700 B/14, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (also 17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily.
She was escorted out by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which parted company arond 1925 B/14.
HMS United was routed through positions 36°08'N, 14°22'E and 37°07'N, 13°15'N. She was to pass through position 'P' at 1200 B/16.
[No log is available for this period so map for this patrol can be displayed.] (50)

15 May 1943 At 2339 B/15, the signal timed 0932 A/15 from the Capt. S.8, was received by HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN). This signal contained the following orders; Proceed from position 'P' to patrol in zones 'B' and 'E' west of 14°00'E. (50)

16 May 1943 At 1200 B/16, when near Marettimo, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted smoke to the south-east. The source of this smoke appeared to proceed to the eastwards of Marettimo Island. (16)

17 May 1943 At 1244 B/17, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted smoke close to the northward of Cape San Vito. Four aircraft were also seen to be patrolling in the area. HMS United closed the smoke to investigate but nothing was sighted.

At 1651 B/17, HMS United sighted an object to the southwest which was closed at speed (submerged). The object was however lost from sight and not seen again. It could not be identified before contact was lost. (16)

18 May 1943 At 0430 B/18, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled on courses 320° / 140° across the convoy route in the middle of the Gulf of Castellammare. Periscope depth patrol was difficult due to the sea state.

At 0935 B/18, smoke was sighted bearing 220°. On coming up to 22 feet the source of the smoke was identified as a merchant vessel of about 2000-tons at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 270° at about 8 knots. Set off to chase the enemy but it was not possible to close and the enemy eventually disappeared in the direction of Cape San Vito. No surface escort was seen but the vessel was escorted by at least two JU.88 aircraft.
At 1839 B/18, in position 38°13'N, 12°59'E, a ship was sighted on bearing 245°. Started attack.

At 1845 B/18, the ship was identified as a Generale-class torpedo boat. Enemy course was 090° at 18 knots. The attack was broken off. Nine minutes later the torpedo boat passed the submarine at a range of 2000 yards.
At 2129 B/18, HMS United surfaced and patrolled up and down moon, close to the northward of the enemy convoy route. (16)

19 May 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) attacked an Italian tug with two torpedoes off Raisi Point, Sicily. Both torpedoes missed. This was probably the Italian tug Tifeo proceeding from Trapani to Palermo.
0435 B/19 - Dived and closed the coast to patrol 4 miles from Raisi Point at the eastern end of the Gulf of Castellammare.
0745 B/19 - Sighted smoke bearing 049°. Started attack and closed at speed for 35 minutes assuming the target's course to be 270°.
0825 B/19 - Identified the target as a 3000-tons hospital ship with a yacht bow and cruiser stern. Enemy course was indeed 270° at 10 knots. HMS United now carried out a dummy attack.
0832 B/19 - The hospital ship passed at a range of 3500 yards. Position was 38°22'N, 13°04'E.
1046 B/19 - Sighted a small vessel bearing 150° proceeding on a course of 090°. Commenced attack.

1114 B/19 - Broke off the attack as the target was now seen to be a small coaster of about 200-tons. This is thought to be a waterboat. The vessel entered the Gulf of Castellammare. Position was 38°12'N, 13°05'E.
1232 B/19 - Sighted a motor A/S boat (MA/SB) proceeding westwards, close inshore. This vessel sounded Raisi Point and proceeded into the Gulf of Castellammare.
1543 B/19 - Started attack on a small vessel sighted close inshore. The enemy was proceeding eastwards round Raisi Point.
1606 B/19 - Broke off the attack when the target was identified as a small drifter of only about 50 tons.
1620 B/19 - Sighted a tug towing a lighter bearing 226°. Started attack. The tug was a modern looking vessel of about 350 tons. Enemy course was 077°, speed 7 knots.
1639 B/19 - In position 38°13'N, 13°05'E fired two torpedoes from 2000 yards. Both torpedoes missed.
1649 B/19 - Retired at speed to seawards.
2011 B/19 - Heard distant depth charging.
2141 B/19 - Surfaced. (16)

20 May 1943 At 0144 B/20, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1130 A/19 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 090° - 43 miles from 'focal point F' [No idea where 'focal point F' was]. This position was 30 miles to the eastwards of HMS United's current position. It was still possible to reach this position during darkness if the battery had been fully charged but this was not yet the case. Course was set to 103° at 5 knots while charging.

At 0208 B/20, an aircraft report was received which reported three motor torpedo boats in position 39°20'N, 14°30'E proceeding on course 220 at 15 knots. This would take these enemy vessels through the position where United was making for at 0530 B/20.
At 0220 B/20, HMS United had to dive for a motor torpedo boat. She surfaced again 20 minutes later.
At 0315 B/20, speed was increased to 9 knots.

At 0446 B/20, HMS United dived in position 38°31'N, 13°27'E, still some 10 miles short of the position ordered and with the battery only half charged. United continued on to the position ordered submerged.

At 0649 B/20, five aircraft were sighted bearing 085°. They were obviously escorting something as they were flying in circles. A barrage balloon was sighted and it was thought the aircraft were escorting a convoy which was making for Palermo.

HMS United went to 70 feet and ran in for 40 minutes at 8.5 knots. After 30 minutes HMS United came to periscope depth. A 3000-tons merchant vessel flying a barrage balloon from aft was sighted. She was escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat. Four Me-110's and one He-115 were flying overhead. The sea was glassy calm which would made a long range attack almost certain to be detected by the air escort. Enemy course was 210°, speed 13 knots.

At 0808 B/20, the attack was broken off as the range could not be closed to less than 5 miles. The battery was now dropping fast so United patrolled up and down the route this convoy had taken at slow speed. Nothing further was however sighted.
At 2141 B/20, HMS United surfaced and charged while patrolling up and down moon.
At 2204 B/20, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1242 A/20 was received in which she was ordered to leave patrol so as to pass through position 'P' at 2200 A/24. (50)

22 May 1943 At 0652 B/22, east-north-east of Cape Zafferano, in position 38°10'N, 13°43'E, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a small minesweeper bearing 286° at a range of 1500 yards. Enemy course was 054°.
At 0658 B/22, the minesweeper changed course to 085°.
At 0820 B/22, three minesweepers were sighted sweeping close inshore on an easterly course. Also two F-lighters were seen proceeding close inshore round Cape Mongerbino towards Palermo.

At 1205 B/22, a small minesweeper or patrol vessel was seen rounding Cape Gallo and it crossed the Bay of Palermo on course 120° at 15 knots. It eventually passed directly over HMS United which had gone deep to evade.
At 1316 B/22, a small drifter of about 50 tons was seen crossing Palermo Bay on course 140°.
At 1439 B/22, when two miles south-east of Cape Gallo another small minesweeper or patrol vessel suddenly rounded the cape and passed overhead at about 15 knots on course 120°.
At 1812 B/22, a small two-masted schooner passed close inshore. This was a suitable gun-target but it was unfortunately in an unsuitable area for gun-action.
At 2133 B/22, HMS United surfaced. (16)
23 May 1943 At 0439 B/23, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled 6 miles to the east of Cape San Vito one mile inshore of the shipping route across Castellammare Bay.

At 1356 B/23, an attack was commenced on a large landing craft. When first sighted HMS United was dead ahead of the vessel which had just rounded Cape San Vito. A retiring turn at speed was carried out to obtain an attack position.

At 1401 B/23, it was seen that the target had the number No. 2777 on it's hull. Enemy course was 090° at about 8 knots. It passed at a range of about 900 yards. Three guns were seen, two being Bofors type. The third gun looked like 3". The cargo was possible petrol or water in tanks. No ensign was seen flying but the crew looked German. Due to the shallow draft of the vessel no torpedo was fired.
At 2127 B/23, HMS United surfaced and set course to pass to the west of Marettimo and return to Malta. (16)
26 May 1943 At 1115 B/26, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (also 17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0810 A/26. (51)

9 Jun 1943 Around 1800 B/9, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (also 18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol of the south and east Calabrian Coast and east coast of Sicily north of 37°44'N.
11 Jun 1943 At 0415 B/11, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived and closed the coast of Sicily near Ripostso to patrol along the coastal shipping route between Ripostso and Taormina.
At 1155 B/11, a small tug towing a lighter was seen close inshore to the north-west. No attack was possible as the enemy was out of range.

At 1738 B/11, in position 37°47'N, 15°17'E, what is thought to be a former French Chasseur was sighted on A/S patrol accompanied by a JU.88. She passed one mile inshore on a southerly course at a speed of 12 knots.
At 2131 B/11, HMS United surfaced and patrolled 12 miles from the shore. (16)

12 Jun 1943 At 0517 B/12, in position 37°53'N, 15°24'E, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted what was first thought to be a submarine bearing 088° at a range of 12000 yards. HMS United was 60° on the starboard bow. Lt. Roxburgh ordered a retiring turn and closed at speed for 10 minutes.

At 0527 B/12, the target was identified as a former French Chasseur escorting a 6000-tons merchant vessel. Lighting conditions made observation difficult but the course of the enemy appeared to be 225° which would take them to Catania. HMS United closed the enemy for 45 minutes.
At 0613 B/12, the range was still 12000 yards and it was now seen that the enemy course was 190°.
At 0654 B/12, in position 37°45'N, 15°22'E, the range had become 16000 yards. The enemy was now seen to alter course to 070°. The attack was now broken off.
At 0733 B/12, the enemy disappeared over the horizon. HMS United set course to close the coast near Riposto to patrol on the coastal shipping route.
At 1003 B/12, an F-lighter and two small schooners passed close inshore proceeding southwards.
At 1421 B/12, three F-lighters in line ahead passed on a southerly course. Again these vessels were close inshore.
At 2134 B/12, HMS United surfaced and patrolled 12 miles from the shore.
At 2318 B/12, the Swedish relief ships Fenris (1904 GRT, built 1941) and Tamara (6390 GRT, built 1931) passed to the northward on an easterly course. (16)

13 Jun 1943 At 0635 B/13, while patrolling about 3 miles from the shore, just to the the east of Cape dell'Armi, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted what was first thought to be a two-funnelled torpedo boat but turned out to be a small open fishing boat with a crew of 6 men. The confusing about the target was due to the mirage effect when the vessel was first seen.
At 1048 B/13, two F-lighters and one small fishing vessel passed close inshore proceeding westwards.
Throughout the day railway traffic along the coast was seen to be considerably less than during HMS United's last patrol in this area, in April 1943.

At 2031 B/13, HMS United had to go deep for a Generali-class torpedo boat which passed across her stern at a range of about 500 yards. Enemy course was 130° at 15 knots. While going deep depth control was lost and the submarine almost broached. Luckily the enemy noticed nothing. (16)

14 Jun 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Ringulv (former French Sainte Marthe, former Norwegian Ringulv (offsite link), 5155 GRT, built 1902) one nautical mile south of Cape dell'Armi in position 37°54'N, 15°42'E. She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba which dropped twelve depth charges on the submarine and picked forty survivors, another seven reached the coast in a lifeboat and twenty-five of the crew were killed or missing.
0238 B/14 - Near Cape dell'Armi a lighted ship was sighted bearing 210°. Enemy course was 360°.
0328 B/14 - The lighted ship was identified as a hospital ship.
0411 B/14 - Dived to avoid being sighted.
0500 B/14 - Identified the hospital ship as the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928). She was stopped in the entrance to the Straits of Messina at a range of 4 miles.
0546 B/14 - Sighted two ships bearing 105°, below the horizon. Altered course towards and commenced attack.

0700 B/14 - Identified the two ships as the Swedish relief ships Akka (5409 GRT, built 1942) and Mongabarra (5279 GRT, built 1941). They too were lying stopped in position 37°48'N, 15°53'E. By this time the Virgilio had gotten underway and was seen to proceed up the Straits of Messina.
0717 B/14 - Altered course to the northward to patrol in the same position as the day before.

1145 B/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel bearing 327°. This vessel was soon identified as an old type merchant vessel of about 5000-tons, in ballast, escorted by a torpedo boat and with two aircraft overhead. Started attack.
1236 B/14 - Fired four torpedoes from 900 yards. 2 hits were obtained and the merchant sank within 5 minutes. After firing the torpedoes Lt. Roxburgh took United down to 120 feet.
1242 B/14 to 1318 B/14 - United was counter attacked by the escorting torpedo boat. In all 20 depth charges were dropped. Close enough to break some 20 lights aboard United.
1416 B/14 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing was in sight except for an aircraft that was circling at the place of the attack. United retreated to the south-west.
1535 B/14 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat and an aircraft to the eastward. They were hunting to the southwards. Range was about 4 miles.
1610 B/14 - The torpedo boat disappeared over the eastern horizon on course 120°. The aircraft was seen still searching to the east and north-east.
1735 B/14 - Sighted an unidentified destroyer or torpedo boat searching to the north-east.
1840 B/14 - The destroyer / torpedo boat disappeared to the north-east. (16)
15 Jun 1943 At 1042 B/15, near Riposto, Sicily, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a small schooner proceeding southwards.
At 1901 B/15, a motor torpedo boat was seen proceeding southwards at speed.
At 2204 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1013 B/15 was received which ordered HMS United to operate east of 16°00'E, in zone 'M' after 0800 B/17. (16)

16 Jun 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a German U-boat to the south-east of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°52'N, 15°44'E. the U-boat was going very fast and a good attack position could not be obtained. Torpedoes were therefore not fired. Shortly afterwards the U-boat made rendezvous with a small Italian patrol vessel and proceeded north to pass through the Straits of Messina.

The U-boat in question was U-593. She had departed Pola in the Adriatic on the 13th to proceed to her patrol area of the North-African coast but a defect had forced her to proceed to Messina for repairs.
0414 B/16 - Dived to patrol off Cape dell'Armi.
0649 B/16 - Sighted a large motor launch type vessel flying the Italian ensign to the northward proceeding on course 210° coming down the Straits of Messina.

0652 B/16 - This vessel suddenly stopped in position 37°53'N, 15°40'E which was about two miles to the westward. She was either a standing patrol instituted as a result of United's attack about 24 hours earlier in this area or she was awaiting the arrival of 'something'.

0752 B/16 - In position 37°52'N, 15°44'E, sighted a U-boat bearing Green 15°, with United 15° on her starboard bow. Enemy course was 270°. Range was less than 2000 yards. HMS United was 500 yards off track. The U-boat showed a large bow wave and was obviously going very fast, later her speed was thought to have been 15 knots. The Italian 'motor launch' joined the submarine soon afterwards and they made off in the direction off Messina.
Lt. Roxburgh tried to get into a firing position but this was not possible. When United have had a stern tube it would have been very difficult to miss the target though given the short range.
0826 B/16 - Resumed patrol to the south of Cape dell'Armi.
1054 B/16 - A lighter passed inshore going westwards.
1906 B/16 - Heard three distant explosions which sounded like torpedo hits.
1910 B/16 - Heard a distant, very heavy explosion, which shook HMS United considerably.
1914 B/16 to 1925 B/16 - Counted about 32 depth charges. It is considered that this was HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) at work. [This was indeed the case.]
2006 B/16 to 2039 B/16 - About 7 more depth charge explosions were heard. (16)
17 Jun 1943 At 0411 B/17, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived and closed the shore to patrol 3 miles from Cape Spartivento.

At 0830 B/17, a small patrol vessel was seen inshore off Cape Spartivento. It was hoped that this vessel might be making rendezvous with 'something', just like the 'motor launch' had done the day before off Cape dell'Armi but nothing developed and the patrol vessel disappeared to the northward not long afterwards.
At 1100 B/17, a distant explosion was heard.
At 1114 B/17, smoke was sighted to the south-south-east. HMS United went deep and ran out to seaward at speed.

At 1130 B/17, the source of the smoke was identified as a merchant vessel that was being escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat. Both were a long way away and well below the horizon and were evidently making for the Straits of Messina. At the same time another distant explosion was heard. These explosions continued at intervals until 1510 B/17. It looked like the torpedo boat was dropping a depth charge at regular half-hourly to hourly intervals.

At 1132 B/17, it was seen that HMS United was now about 70° on the starboard bow of the merchant vessel, so she went deep again and ran in at speed on a broad track in the hopes of getting close enough for a torpedo attack from long range.

At 1202 B/17, on coming up for a look, the merchant vessel was seen to be dead ahead at a range of 8 miles on a course of about 305°. Enemy speed was approximately 8 knots. Position was 37°45'N, 15°55'E.

At 1215 B/17, the attack was broken off and HMS United set course to patrol off the coast near Cape Spartivento again as it was hoped that the appearance of the patrol craft earlier that morning might herald the approach of 'something' but no more shipping was sighted all day. (16)

18 Jun 1943 At 0745 B/18, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat passing to the southward. Bearing 169°, range 11000 yards. This put this vessel in position 37°39'N, 16°04'E. Enemy course was about 100° at a speed of at least 15 knots but most probably a little more.
As it was thought this torpedo boat may be en-route to meet a merchant vessel is was decided to patrol in position 180° - Cape Spartivento - 15 miles but nothing further was sighted all day. (16)

18 Jun 1943 In a signal timed 0830 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) and HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN); 1) United is to leave patrol to pass through position 37°23'N, 16°02'E at 2000 B/21 thence through positions 36°26'N, 15°43'E and 36° 26'N, 14°20'E to arrive at the Malta West Sanctuary rendezvous at 0830 B/23. 2) Unison is to leave patrol so as to follow the same route 24 hours later. (52)
19 Jun 1943 At 0419 B/19, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled once more 15 miles to the south of Cape Spartivento.
At 0610 B/19, a destroyer was seen bearing 278° at a range of 6 miles.

At 0643 B/19, the destroyer was identified as a Spica-class torpedo boat, which passed in position 37°42'N, 16°02'E at a range of 1200 yards proceeding on a course of 090° at 20 knots. Hopes were once more raised of seeing a merchant vessel later on in the day but nothing developed in the end.

Between 1303 B/19 and 1305 B/19, a single distant explosion was heard followed shortly after by ten further explosions. This may have been a torpedo hit followed by two patterns of five depth charges east. [Around this time an Allied air attack took place of Reggio Calabria and this may have accounted for the explosions heard.] (16)

20 Jun 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian armed merchant cruiser D 7 / Olbia (3514 GRT, built 1929) about 20 nautical miles south of Cape Spartivento, Italy in position 37°40'N, 16°02'E. She was on a trip from Naples to Brindisi and was hit by two torpedoes. The corvette Gabbiano was sailed to recover the survivors.
0414 B/20 - Dived to patrol once more 15 miles to the south of Cape Spartivento.
1442 B/20 - Sighted a merchant vessel to the westward on an easterly course. Started attack.

1454 B/20 - The merchant vessel appeared to be unescorted which was disturbing as it meant it may likely be a hospital ship. However Lt. Roxburgh noticed that the vessel was zigzagging which indicated that this may not be the case. HMS United went deep again and ran in as speed.

1505 B/20 - Came up for another look and saw that the merchant vessel was not a hospital ship but that she was camouflaged and had a large gun forward which was thought to be a 6" one. The enemy vessel had done a zig towards putting the submarine 20° on the port bow on a 60° track. Her speed was 15 knots. She looked like a modern liner with a raked bow and was flying the Italian ensign. This was thus thought to be an Italian armed merchant cruiser of between 7000 to 10000 tons.

1513 B/20 - In position 37°40'N, 16°02'E fired four torpedoes from 900 yards. Three hits were claimed with explosions being heard at 46, 57 and 67 seconds after firing the first torpedo. As the submarine had lost trim after firing the explosions were not observed but on regaining periscope depth about 3 minutes later the ship was seen to be stopped, enveloped in smoke from the bridge aft and burning furiously with a list to starboard. A large hole was seen blown out of the stem on the waterline and two fires were seen, one under the bridge and one aft. The Italians were seen to abandon ship within 5 minutes from being hit. HMS United retired deep from the scene of the attack at speed after Lt. Roxburgh had given the entire crew the opportunity to look at the sinking enemy vessel through the periscope, a good morale booster.
1545 B/20 - Came to periscope depth for a look. The enemy vessel was still blazing furiously but showed no sign of sinking.
1606 B/20 - A very loud explosion was heard while being deep. Went straight up to periscope depth to have a good look. The enemy vessel was no longer seen and she had undoubtedly sunk.
2130 B/20 - Surfaced.

2325 B/20 - Passed United's signal timed 2259 B/20 reporting the expenditure of the torpedoes and the expected time of arrival at Malta. This signal was acknowledged by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 0130 B/21 which was received at 0232 B/21. (16)
22 Jun 1943 At 1115 B/22, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (also 18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski), were escorted in by minesweepers [unidentified] which had joined at 0830 B/22. (16)

7 Jul 1943 At 2000 B/7, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (also 19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto in a patrol line with four other submarines.

The four other submarines in this patrol line were; HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN). HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) had been added to them temporarily to fill the gap left by the delayed sailing of HMS United.
The sailing of HMS United had been delayed by four / five days due to Lt. Roxburgh being sick and no spare Commanding Officers being available at Malta to take over.
HMS United was routed as follows; 36°28'N, 15°32'E at 0330 B/8 thence 38°40'N, 17°44'E at 0430 B/10 thence to her patrol position in the Gulf of Taranto.
9 Jul 1943 At 0940 B/9, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted smoke bearing 352°. Also a Cant. Z.506B was seen patrolling. Closed at speed to investigate.

At 1030 B/9, the mast, bridge and funnel of a merchant vessel, escorted by two destroyer / torpedo boats were seen. The Cant seaplane was also still patrolling overhead. Enemy course was 180° at about 10 knots. All three vessels were hull down. Their course took them due south from Punta Stilo. Continued to close at speed.

At 1050 B/9, the enemy convoy altered course to 240° in position 37°58'N, 16°39'E and passed out of range. Their range at that moment was about 6 to 7 miles. Their new course would take them through the focal point 15 miles due south of Cape Spartivento.
At 1103 B/9, the attack was broken off. (16)
11 Jul 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) arrived in her assigned patrol area in the Gulf of Taranto.
At 2222 B/11, a small dark object was sighted to the southward which could not be identified. It was soon lost from sight and not seen again. (16)

12 Jul 1943 At 2316 B/12, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a small dark object to the southwest. This was considered to may have been HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) which was in the adjacent area. (16)
13 Jul 1943 At 1046 B/13, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a schooner to the westward proceeding northwards.
At 1125 B/13, a second schooner was seen to the westwards. This schooner was also proceeding to the north.
At 1819 B/13, an A/S schooner was seen patrolling in position 39°25'N, 17°24'W. This schooner was lost from sight to the north-west at 2007 B/13.
At 2100 B/13, a small vessel was seen inshore on bearing 290°. Course of this vessel was northbound. The vessel could not be positively identified. (16)
14 Jul 1943 At 0656 B/14, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted the same A/S schooner as yesterday in position 39.28'N, 17.24'E patrolling north and south.
At 0938 B/14, the A/S schooner was lost from sight to the northward.
Between 1555 B/14 and 1605 B/14 about seven fairly loud distant explosions were heard. [HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) torpedoed and sank a schooner at this time.]
At 1932 B/14, the masts of an A/S schooner were seen. This schooner was patrolling to the westward. (16)

15 Jul 1943 The Italian (transport) submarine Remo was torpedoed and sunk in the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°19'N, 17°30'E by the British submarine HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN). She was on a trip from Taranto to Naples and this was her first operational sortie. Fitfty-five were killed, her commanding officer, T.V. Salvatore Vassalo, the navigating officer and two ratings were rescued by HMS United.

0508 B/15 - East-south-east of Punta Alice, in position 39°18'N, 17°30'E, sighted what was thought to be a former French Chasseur passing at a range of 1800 yards. She was going very slowly and is thought to have been on a listening sweep. It was considered possible that an aircraft which passed overhead at 0205 hours and which had forces United to dive, had reported the submarine.
1809 B/15 - Sighted a very small object about 4 nautical miles away.
1814 B/15 - The object was identified as a submarine. Started attack.
1825 B/15 - The submarine was identified as being Italian.

1831 B/15 - Fired four torpedoes from 500 yards. Two hits were obtained. Shortly after the second torpedo hit the stern of the submarine rose out of the water at a 60-degree angle and she sank in four seconds.
1836 B/15 - Four survivors were seen swimming in the water.
1849 B/15 - United surfaced and picked them up. The survivors turned out to be the Commanding Officer, a midshipman (who was the Navigating Officer) and two seamen.
1852 B/15 - Dived.
1935 B/15 - Went deep to reload.
2053 B/15 - Completed the reload and returned to periscope depth.
2142 B/15 - Surfaced.

2230 B/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 B/15 in which HMS United was ordered to leave patrol on 20 July so as to make rendezvous off Malta with the minesweeper at 1000 B/23. The return route had already been signalled in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1345 B/13 but this signal had not been received. As Lt. Roxburgh feared more important orders may have been missed he decided to withdraw to the south-east and break W/T silence.
2330 B/15 - Commenced retiring the the south-east at speed. (16)

16 Jul 1943 At 0245 B/16, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) commenced attempting to pass her signal timed 0151 B/16 in which she reported to non-receipt of the signal timed 1435 B/15. Also the sinking of the enemy submarine was reported.
At 0453 B/16, HMS United dived in position 38°50'N, 17°55'E some 27 miles to the south-east of her patrol position / area.

At 2217 B/16, in position 39°19’N, 17°40’E, HMS United sighted a submarine making a lot of smoke. As it was inside the area supposedly occupied by HMS Trooper or HMS Tactician, the submarine refrained from attack. In fact, this was the Italian Beilul proceeding from Leros to Taranto. Her diesels were making excessive smoke and upon spotting the British submarine, T.V. Pasquale Beltrame prudently ordered to switch to electric motors. He was uncertain if it was friend of foe and decided to move away.

At 2328 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0536 B/16 was received which acknowledged the receipt of HMS United's signal timed 0152 B/16. It also stated that the signal timed 1435 B/13 would be repeated.

At 2332 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0630 B/16 which repeated his signal timed 1435 B/16 and also stated that this signal had never been transmitted to submarines in error. In this signal HMS United was also ordered to occupy 'area 5' at 1200 B/17. (16)

17 Jul 1943 At 0705 B/17, in position 39°28’N, 17°24’E, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted the foretop and funnels of a cruiser below the horizon, bearing 130° at a range of 7 to 8 miles.

At 0715 B/17, the cruiser, which was identified as a light cruiser of the Regolo-class, was on course 355° at 30-32 knots. It passed at a range of about 8000 yards from the submarine, too far for any attack.
This was the Scipione Africano on her way to Taranto after running the gauntlet of the Straits of Messina.
At 0905 B/17, a Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted proceeding inshore. The enemy vessel was southbound and below the horizon and proceeding very fast.
At 0950 B/17, a second Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted. Also inshore and below the horizon. This torpedo boat was also southbound, below the horizon and proceeding very fast. (16)

18 Jul 1943 At 1206 B/18, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), which was patrolling between Punta Alice and Cape Colonne, sighted a 100-tons schooner close inshore. The enemy vessel was northbound.
At 1330 B/18, another small schooner was seen close inshore proceeding northwards.
At 1430 B/18, the 100-tons schooner, which had been in sighted the whole time, commenced patrolling on an east-west line off Punta Alice, so presumably it was an A/S schooner.
At 1640 B/18, the A/S schooner was lost from sight to the northward. (16)
19 Jul 1943 At 0710 B/19, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a corvette-type of patrol vessel proceeding southwards at speed.
At 0721 B/19, when off Crotone, the vessel altered course to 277° and made for the breakwater.
At 0820 B/19, HMS United went deep for a Cant. Z.506B which shortly afterwards passed overhead.

At 0830 B/19, on returning to periscope depth it was seen that the submarine was dead ahead of a slow moving torpedo boat which was still 4 miles away. The torpedo boat was alone and was doing a careful A/S sweep transmitting on Asdic. Enemy course was 015° at a speed of only 4 knots. Lt. Roxburgh took United deep and kept stern on. The torpedo boat later passed very close astern and then altered course to the east, crossed the bow of the submarine and then altered course again to the south but then drew away clear, without gaining contact.
At 1010 B/19, HMS United returned to periscope depth to find nothing in sight.

At 1114 B/19, a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen conducting an A/S sweep to the southward on course 015°. Presumably she was the one encountered earlier that morning. She appeared to be sweeping an area off Crotone from Cape Colonne northwards for 5 miles. HMS United retired towards Punta Alice.
At 2158 B/19, the HE of a destroyer or torpedo boat doing 28 knots was picked up. This vessel was proceeding southwards and passed to the eastward of the submarine. Nothing was sighted though.

At 2359 B/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1620 B/19 was received which contained the following; Cancel my signal timed 1145 B/15. Leave patrol and return to Malta routed as follows: 39°07'N, 17°25'E at 0100 B/21, 38°41'N, 17°45'E at 0600/21, 37°00'N, 17°45'E at 1200/22, 036°17'N, 17°26'E at 0100 B/23, thence direct to Malta east of searched channels to make rendezvous with a minesweeper at 0800 B/24. Proceed submerged by day. Many enemy submarines are at sea. (53)
20 Jul 1943 At 0724 B/20, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted an A/S schooner stopped to the northward in position 39°20'N, 17°15'E.
At 0740 B/20, a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen close inshore bearing 215°. This torpedo boat was on an A/S sweep and proceeding northwards.
At 0800 B/20, the torpedo boat was seen to alter course to the southward in position 39°14'N, 17°12'E, while continuing the A/S sweep.
At 0809 B/20, the torpedo boat was lost from sight.
At 0922 B/20, HMS United altered course to 175° when 5000 yards from the stopped schooner. At this time a second schooner was sighted patrolling to seaward in position 39°23'N, 17°17'E.
At 1040 B/20, two small schooners passed close inshore on a northerly course.
At 1356 B/20, one schooner passed inshore. This vessel was southbound.
At 2143 B/20, two small dark objects were sighted dead astern. It was believed this were patrol vessels. Lt. Roxburgh kept the submarines stern on while slowly withdrawing.

At 2145 B/20, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1117 B/20 was received which contained the following; Own surface craft are operating off Crotone tonight. Leave the area two hours earlier than time ordered in my signal timed 1620 B/19.

This signal referred to the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Commodore W.G. Agnew, CB, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. G.D. Belben, DSC, AM, RN) and the destroyers HMS Troubridge (Capt. C.L. Firth, MVO, RN), HMS Tumult (Lt.Cdr. N. Lanyon, RN), HMS Quilliam (Capt. S.H. Carlill, DSO, RN), HMS Petard (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Egan, RN), HMS Offa (Lt.Cdr. R.F. Leonard, RN) and ORP Piorun (Cdr. S.T. Dzienisiewicz) which departed Malta at 1330 B/20 for a bombardment of Crotone.
The bombardment was carried out shortly before 0200 B/21. Main targets were the harbour area and a chemical plant.
The force returned to Malta around 1600 B/21. (54)

21 Jul 1943 At 0256 B/21, well to the east-south-east of Cape Rizzuto, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) heard fast HE, probably from a motor torpedo boat bearing 310° drawing up the starboard side. Nothing was sighted though.
After 8 minutes the HE disappeared on bearing 250°. (16)
24 Jul 1943 At 1145 B/24, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (also 19th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR), which had joined around 0900 B/24. (16)

15 Aug 1943 At 1930 B/15, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (also 20th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto, off Punta Alice.

17 Aug 1943 At 1020 B/17, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 050° at a range of 8 miles. Course was altered towards and the other submarine was challenged and the correct reply was received. The other submarine was HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) which was en-route to Malta. (16)

18 Aug 1943 At 0745 B/18, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat aground close to the north of Alice Point. There appeared to be no signs of activity onboard and no attempts were being made to re-float her.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Lince which had grounded on 4 August 1943.
At 0918 B/18, three motor anti-submarine boats (MA/SB's) were seen inshore proceeding northwards in line ahead. (16)

19 Aug 1943 At 1830 B/19, while patrolling 3 to 4 miles to the east of Punta Alice, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), sighted two southbound large motor launch type patrol vessels (possibly former French Chasseurs).
At 2155 B/19, just after surfacing, a small dark object was sighted inshore. This object was proceeding southwards and fast HE was picked up. It was considered to be a motor torpedo boat. (16)
20 Aug 1943 At 0830 B/20, while patrolling about 3 miles east of Punta Alice, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a schooner close inshore proceeding on a northerly course.

At 1005 B/20, an attack was commenced on what appeared to be a northbound merchant vessel escorted by two motor torpedo boats. Unfortunately the contact turned out to be the foc'sle, bridge and after superstructure of a former French Chasseur or large motor launch. A second similar vessel was seen later. They rounded Alice Point and continued on course 320° up the coastal route.
At 1115 B/20, a vessel, thought to be a motor A/S vessel (MA/SB) passed inshore close to the north of Punta Alice.
At 1602 B/20, a schooner was seen proceeding inshore close to the north of Punta Alice.
At 2229 B/20, HE of a motor torpedo boat was heard astern and a small object was seen close ashore. The enemy was southbound. (16)
21 Aug 1943 At 1539 B/21, while patrolling about 3.5 miles from Punta Alice, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a small southbound schooner close inshore.

As this was now the fourth day in a row that no targets of any size were seen, Lt. Roxburgh decided to patrol further to the seawards, 12 miles to the east of Alice Point, for the next couple of days. (16)

22 Aug 1943 At 0217 B/22, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) heard the HE of motor torpedo boats and saw two southbound motor torpedo boats proceeding at high speed about five miles from Punta Alice. As HMS United was up moon she dived until the motor torpedo boats were clear surfacing again at 0235 B/22.
At 2310 B/22, the HE of a northbound motor torpedo boat was heard proceeding at speed. The enemy passed to the westward. (16)

23 Aug 1943 At 2117 B/23, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1120 B/23, which contained the following orders; Leave patrol to pass through positions 39°25'N, 17°17'E at 0600 B/27, 38°56'N, 18°34'E at 0300 B/28th and 35°45'N, 16°38'E at 1231 B/29 to make rendezvous with the minesweeper 5 miles to the east of the end of eastern swept channel at 0800 B/30. Surface passage on the 28th. (55)

24 Aug 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted nothing all day while patrolling 12 miles to the east of Punta Alice. Lt. Roxburgh decided to spend the last two days on patrol on the coastal route of Punta Alice. (16)

26 Aug 1943 HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted noting on her last full day in the area off Alice Point. Lt. Roxburgh intended to fire a torpedo at the grounded Italian torpedo boat Lince first seen on 18 August. The vessel was however high and dry on the beach and during the nine days in the area there were no signs of any salvage activity. So he concluded that the vessel was actually not worth the torpedo. (16)
27 Aug 1943 At 0600 B/27, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1120 B/23. (16)
29 Aug 1943 At 2308 B/29, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) encountered HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) in position 35°48'N, 16°02'E and replied to her challenge.
HMS Unshaken had seen HMS United at 2305 B/29 and was thus the first one to make the challenge. She reported the encounter in position 35°49.5'N, 16°02'E (56)
30 Aug 1943 At 1100 B/30, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (also 20th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
This had been HMS United's last patrol in the Mediterranean. She was now to return to the U.K. to refit. (16)
8 Sep 1943 Around 1030 B/8, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for passage to the U.K. where she was to refit.
She was in company with HMS Unison (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) which was also to return to the U.K. The submarines were escorted to the rendezvous with convoy MKS 24 by HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR).
For the daily positions of HMS United during this passage see the map below.
10 Sep 1943 At 1050 B/10, in position 37°17'N, 07°48'E, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) was passed by the Italian Battlefleet on her way to surrender at Malta. (16)
13 Sep 1943 Around 2040 B/13, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (16)

16 Sep 1943 Around 0800 B/16, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 21st war patrol. She was ordered to proceed to the U.K. En-route she was to patrol in the Bay of Biscay for a short anti-U-boat patrol.

On leaving Gibraltar, HMS United was routed as follows; Position 'B', 35°49'N, 06°06'W at 1320 B/16. Position 'V', 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 1430 B/16. Position 'T', 36°37'N, 07°24'W at 0001 B/17. Position 'Q', 36°51'N, 09°13'W at 2230 B/17. Position 'R', 38°50'N, 10°01'W at 0030 B/19 and Position 'O', 43°01'N, 10°01'W at 0530 B/21. Further onward routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).
